content: Let me start by reading the necessary files to understand my task as the lean-vs-blueprint-checker subagent.
I'll read the subagent instructions and project context first, then examine the Lean file and blueprint chapter.

1	---
2	name: lean-vs-blueprint-checker
3	description: Per-file bidirectional verifier. Reads one .lean file and its blueprint chapter; reports (a) whether the Lean file follows the blueprint — fake/placeholder statements, signature mismatches with \lean{...} hints, proof divergence, "temporary" defs — AND (b) whether the blueprint chapter is detailed enough to have guided a faithful formalization of this file.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [review]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - I am highly recommended in the review phase. Dispatch one checker
11	    per `.lean` file that received prover work this iteration. Each
12	    dispatch is independent and parallel-able.
13	
14	    **You may skip me this iter when NO `.lean` file received prover
15	    work** (e.g. the prover phase was skipped, or every prover
16	    INCOMPLETE'd without committing edits). Record under `## Subagent
17	    skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`:
18	    ``- lean-vs-blueprint-checker: no .lean files modified this iter
19	    (no prover edits to verify)``.
20	
21	    Do NOT skip a per-file dispatch when the prover DID commit edits
22	    to that file — skipping a prover-touched file is the review-phase
23	    failure this affordance exists to surface, not to enable. The
24	    "highly recommended" framing applies to the whole-iter decision,
25	    not to picking-and-choosing within an iter that has multiple
26	    prover-touched files.
27	  - Also dispatch on any file a code-audit subagent in your catalog
28	    flagged as suspicious, even if it received no prover work this
29	    iter.
30	  - The directive must name exactly one Lean file and its blueprint
31	    chapter — no global context, no strategy snapshot. My value is
32	    the narrow file-vs-chapter view.
33	  - I report bidirectionally: Lean → blueprint AND blueprint → Lean.
34	    The blueprint can be the failure (too thin to guide
35	    formalization, missing the level of detail the Lean code clearly
36	    needed), not only the Lean. If I flag a chapter as inadequate,
37	    the plan agent should dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing
38	    subagent to address it.
39	  - Any must-fix-this-iter finding I report blocks downstream work on
40	    the affected file/chapter until addressed. Soft enforcement: the
41	    plan agent reads my report and follows the gate rules in plan.md.
42	---
43	
44	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker
45	
46	You compare **one Lean file** against **its blueprint chapter** and report whether the Lean follows the blueprint faithfully. You are read-only.
47	
48	## Your Job
49	
50	Your directive names one `.lean` file (e.g. `Algebra/WLocal.lean`) and its corresponding blueprint chapter (e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex`). You audit **bidirectionally**:
51	
52	### (A) Does the Lean follow the blueprint?
53	
54	For each `\lean{...}`-tagged block in the chapter, verify the Lean side:
55	
56	- **Declaration exists** with the right name (matches `\lean{namespace.theorem_name}`).
57	- **Signature matches** the informal statement in the chapter — the types are what the prose says they should be. A statement that type-checks but uses the wrong type class (e.g. `Smooth f` where the prose says "smooth of relative dimension n") is **wrong**, not "approximate".
58	- **Proof follows the blueprint sketch** — the proof in the Lean file should be a formalization of the steps in the chapter's `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}`, not a different argument that happens to produce the same conclusion.
59	- **No fake / placeholder statements** — definitions or theorems whose body is just `:= sorry` or a trivially-true tautology while the chapter prose claims something substantive.
60	- **No "temporary" definitions left in place** — comments like `-- TODO: replace with the real definition`, `-- placeholder until we figure out X`, or definitions whose body is suspicious (e.g. `def Foo := True`, weakened-wrong definitions standing in for the real concept).
61	- **No comments that explain away wrong code** — "we are using a wrong definition for now", "this proof is incomplete but will be fixed", etc. These are red flags, not acceptable workflow notes.
62	
63	### (B) Is the blueprint adequate for this Lean file?
64	
65	The blueprint can also be the failure mode. For the chapter you're reading, check:
66	
67	- **Every Lean declaration in this file should map to a blueprint block** (via `\lean{...}`) OR be an explicitly-helper-only declaration. If the file has substantive declarations the blueprint doesn't reference at all, the chapter is incomplete.
68	- **Proof sketches in the blueprint should be detailed enough** for a prover to formalize correctly. If you see Lean proofs that clearly went through significant reasoning the blueprint's `\begin{proof}` does NOT preview, flag the chapter as under-specified.
69	- **`\lean{...}` hints should pin the Lean target precisely.** If the blueprint says "smooth morphism" without specifying which Mathlib predicate (`Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n` vs `IsSmooth`), and the Lean file ended up with the wrong one, that's BOTH a Lean error AND a blueprint clarity failure. Flag both sides.
70	- **Definitions in the blueprint should be at the right level of generality** for the Lean to consume. If the blueprint defines something narrower than the project actually needs and the Lean had to write a parallel API to cover the gap, that's a blueprint adequacy failure.
71	
72	Both directions matter. Don't excuse a wrong Lean statement by saying "the blueprint is loose"; don't excuse a thin blueprint by saying "the Lean works anyway." Report both.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Lean file
83	<path/to/file.lean>
84	
85	## Blueprint chapter
86	blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex
87	
88	## Known issues
89	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
90	```
91	
92	That's it. No strategy snapshot, no references, no full project context — just the two files and any pre-known issues to skip. Cross-file consistency is the job of the code-audit and blueprint-review subagents in the catalog; you stay focused on this one pair.
93	
94	## What you do
95	
96	1. Read your directive.
97	2. Read the Lean file completely. Note every declaration (`def`, `theorem`, `lemma`, `instance`, `axiom`, …) and its signature.
98	3. Read the blueprint chapter completely. List every `\lean{...}` reference and its corresponding declaration block.
99	4. **For each `\lean{...}` block**: locate the Lean declaration. Verify:
100	   - It exists.
101	   - Its signature matches the informal statement (use `archon-lean-lsp` for hover/signature info).
102	   - If the Lean has a body (proof or definition body), does it match the blueprint's prose? Naming and tactic choice can differ; the *mathematical content* should match.
103	5. **For each declaration in the Lean file**: is it `\lean{...}`-referenced from the blueprint? Unreferenced declarations are notable but not necessarily wrong (helpers may exist). Flag suspect ones.
104	6. **Scan for red flags** in the Lean file:
105	   - `:= sorry` where the blueprint claims a substantive statement (placeholder).
106	   - Suspect bodies like `:= True`, `:= rfl` for non-trivial claims, `:= Classical.choice _`.
107	   - Comments excusing wrong/incomplete code: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- will fix later`, `-- this is wrong but works for now`.
108	   - `axiom` declarations.
109	7. **Cross-check** any `import` paths and namespace usage that should match what the blueprint's `\lean{...}` hints suggest.
110	
111	You may use:
112	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics.
113	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to count sorries in the file.
114	
115	You may NOT modify the Lean file, the blueprint, or any other project file. Even when a fix is obvious.
116	
117	## Report format
118	
119	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
120	
121	```markdown
122	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Check Report
123	
124	## Slug
125	<slug>
126	
127	## Iteration
128	<NNN>
129	
130	## Files audited
131	- Lean: <path>
132	- Blueprint: <path>
133	
134	## Per-declaration
135	
136	For every `\lean{...}` block in the chapter, one entry:
137	
138	### `\lean{Foo.bar}` (chapter: \thm:bar)
139	- **Lean target exists**: yes / no (if no: declaration name in the .lean that comes closest, or "none found")
140	- **Signature matches**: yes / partial / no (with one line on the mismatch)
141	- **Proof follows sketch**: yes / partial / no / N/A (if N/A: chapter doesn't have a proof body to compare against)
142	- **notes**: <free-form, one line per observation>
143	
144	### `\lean{Foo.baz}` ...
145	
146	## Red flags
147	
148	(Section appears only if there are findings.)
149	
150	### Placeholder / suspect bodies
151	- `Foo.bar` at line 42: body is `:= sorry` but blueprint claims a substantive proof.
152	- `Foo.qux` at line 78: defined as `:= True`, suspect.
153	- ...
154	
155	### Excuse-comments
156	- `Foo.lean:113`: comment "TODO: replace with real def" attached to `Foo.frob`. Blueprint claims `Foo.frob` is the actual definition, not a placeholder.
157	- ...
158	
159	### Axioms / Classical.choice on non-trivial claims
160	- `Foo.lean:200`: `axiom` declaration `Foo.skip_step` introduced. Blueprint does not authorize this.
161	
162	## Unreferenced declarations (informational)
163	
164	<list of declarations in the Lean file that no `\lean{...}` references. Most are helpers — only flag the ones whose name suggests they should be in the blueprint.>
165	
166	## Blueprint adequacy for this file
167	
168	A bidirectional check: does the blueprint chapter give a prover enough detail to formalize this file correctly?
169	
170	- **Coverage**: <N>/<M> Lean declarations have a corresponding `\lean{...}` block in the chapter. Unreferenced declarations: <count> helpers (acceptable) + <count> substantive (flagged below).
171	- **Proof-sketch depth**: <one of: adequate / under-specified / silent>. If under-specified or silent, list which `\thm:...` blocks need expansion.
172	- **Hint precision**: <one of: precise / loose / wrong>. Loose hints (the prose pins one Mathlib predicate but the `\lean{...}` doesn't, leaving the prover to guess) get flagged. Wrong hints (the `\lean{...}` names a declaration whose signature doesn't match the prose) are critical.
173	- **Generality**: <one of: matches need / too narrow / too broad>. If too narrow, name the parallel API that the project ended up writing because the blueprint didn't cover the needed level of generality.
174	- **Recommended chapter-side actions**: <bullet list of items a blueprint-writing subagent should land if you flagged anything above>
175	
176	## Severity summary
177	
178	Apply these rules verbatim. Do NOT under-classify to soften the blow — the plan agent's gates depend on accurate severity, and a hidden critical finding hardens into shipped wrong code.
179	
180	- **must-fix-this-iter** — every one of the following lands here, no exceptions:
181	  - Placeholder body (`:= sorry`, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim, suspect `:= Classical.choice _` patterns) on a declaration the blueprint claims is substantive.
182	  - Signature mismatch with the blueprint's prose — including using the wrong Mathlib predicate (e.g. `Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n`) when the prose pins one specific predicate.
183	  - Excuse-comments (`-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- placeholder`, `-- wrong but works for now`) on declarations the blueprint claims are real.
184	  - Axioms or `Classical.choice _` on substantive claims the blueprint does NOT authorize.
185	  - Blueprint adequacy failure: chapter is so under-specified that a prover could not have formalized this file correctly from prose alone.
186	  - Weakened-wrong definitions: Lean defines a structurally-different stand-in (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic Γ(X,⊤)` when the blueprint defines it as an invertible quasi-coherent O_X-module).
187	- **major** — partial signature mismatches that are fixable in-place, missing `\lean{...}` references to declarations that the blueprint should reference, comments that are stale but not actively misleading.
188	- **minor** — naming drift, helpers worth promoting to blueprint blocks, low-impact prose-vs-Lean differences.
189	
190	Overall verdict: one sentence.
191	```
192	
193	The plan agent's per-file gate (see plan.md and the blueprint-review subagent's `dispatcher_notes` in the catalog) treats every must-fix-this-iter finding as blocking. If you classify a wrong signature as "major" to avoid the gate firing, you are working against the project, not for it.
194	
195	## Return value
196	
197	Your final assistant message:
198	
199	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> declarations checked, <M> red flags`
200	- The path to your full report.
201	
202	## Reminders
203	
204	- **One file pair, no global context.** Cross-file consistency belongs to the catalog's code-audit and blueprint-review subagents.
205	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
206	- **Excuse-comments are red flags, not workflow.** Treat "we use a wrong def for now" with the suspicion it deserves.
207	- **Mathematical content match, not syntactic.** The Lean proof can choose different tactics than the chapter's sketch implies, as long as the mathematical steps are the same.
208

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

8	/-!
9	# The Quot scheme: Quot-foundations layer
10	
11	This file packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
12	Grothendieck–Altman–Kleiman Quot-scheme construction. It introduces the
13	per-fiber Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf, the Quot functor
14	`Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` of `T`-flat coherent quotients, and the Grassmannian
15	*scheme* (Mathlib at the pinned commit carries only a linear-algebra
16	Grassmannian) together with its representability.
17	
18	The 4 blueprint-pinned declarations are:
19	
20	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial` (def) — the
21	   **Hilbert polynomial function** `s ↦ Φ_{F,s} ∈ ℚ[λ]` of a coherent
22	   sheaf `F` on `X` over a finite-type `π : X ⟶ S` with respect to a
23	   line bundle `L`. Encoded as a function `S → Polynomial ℚ`.
24	
25	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor` (def) — the **Quot
26	   functor** `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S} : (Sch/S)^op ⥤ Set` sending an
27	   `S`-scheme `T ⟶ S` to the set of equivalence classes
28	   `⟨F, q⟩` of pairs `(F, q)` with `F` a `T`-flat coherent sheaf on
29	   `X_T`, `q : E_T ↠ F` a surjection, and `F|_{X_t}` having Hilbert
30	   polynomial `Φ` at every `t ∈ T`.
31	
32	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian` (def) — the
33	   **Grassmannian functor** `Grass(V, d) : (Sch/S)^op ⥤ Set` of
34	   rank-`d` quotients of a locally free `O_S`-module `V`.
35	
36	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian.representable` (theorem)
37	   — the **representability of the Grassmannian** by a smooth projective
38	   `S`-scheme `Gr_S(V, d)` of relative dimension `d(r-d)`, equipped with
39	   the Plücker closed embedding into `ℙ_S(⋀^d V)`.
40	
41	## Note on type expressivity
42	
43	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof",
44	each declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
45	
46	- `hilbertPolynomial` returns `Polynomial ℚ` keyed by `s : S`, not
47	  `Unit`; the Hilbert polynomial is a non-trivial invariant of the
48	  coherent sheaf at the fiber over `s`.
49	- `QuotFunctor` and `Grassmannian` return contravariant functors into
50	  `Type u` — substantive presheaves of sets, not constant functors.
51	- `Grassmannian.representable` packages the
52	  `Functor.RepresentableBy` Yoneda-bijection structure: existence of a
53	  scheme `Y` together with a `RepresentableBy Y` witness — substantive
54	  content (a representable functor is determined by its representing
55	  object up to canonical isomorphism, and the witness is the data of
56	  that isomorphism family).
57	
58	## Mathlib status
59	
60	Mathlib (master `b80f227`) provides:
61	- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules` (the category `X.Modules`),
62	- `Scheme.Modules.pullback`, `Scheme.Modules.pushforward` (the
63	  pullback–pushforward adjunction at level `i = 0`),
64	- `CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy` for representable functors,
65	- `AlgebraicGeometry.LocallyOfFiniteType`, `AlgebraicGeometry.IsLocallyNoetherian`
66	  (morphism / object property predicates), and
67	- `Polynomial` for `ℚ[λ]`.
68	
69	Mathlib does NOT provide (at the pinned commit):
70	- a Grassmannian *scheme* (only a linear-algebra Grassmannian
71	  as a finite-rank-quotient variety),
72	- the Quot/Hilbert functor or its representability,
73	- Snapper's Lemma for the polynomial property of Euler characteristics.
74	
75	## References
76	
77	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_QuotScheme.tex`. Source:
78	Nitsure, "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", §1 (FGA Explained
79	Ch. 5, arXiv:math/0504020 pp. 5–35); cf. Hartshorne III.5.2.
80	-/
81	
82	set_option autoImplicit false
83	
84	universe u
85	
86	open CategoryTheory Limits

88	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
89	
90	namespace Scheme
91	
92	/-! ## §1. Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf
93	
94	For a finite-type morphism `π : X ⟶ S` with `S` noetherian and a coherent
95	sheaf `F` on `X` whose schematic support is proper over `S` (here encoded
96	as plain `X.Modules` for the file-skeleton), the per-fiber Hilbert
97	polynomial is the function
98	
99	`s ↦ Φ_{F,s} ∈ ℚ[λ],   Φ_{F,s}(m) = χ(X_s, F|_{X_s} ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})`.
100	
101	Snapper's Lemma ensures this is a polynomial in `m`; the proof requires
102	graded-Euler-characteristic infrastructure and is not stated here.
103	
104	Blueprint reference: `def:hilbert_polynomial` (Nitsure §1; cf. Hartshorne
105	III.5.2). -/
106	
107	/-- The **Hilbert polynomial** of a coherent sheaf `F` on `X` over `S` at
108	the fiber over `s ∈ S` with respect to a line bundle `L` on `X`.
109	
110	Encoded as a function `s ↦ Φ_{F,s} ∈ ℚ[λ]`. The defining formula
111	
112	`Φ_{F,s}(m) = χ(X_s, F|_{X_s} ⊗ L_s^{⊗ m})
113	            = Σ_i (-1)^i dim_{κ(s)} H^i(X_s, F|_{X_s} ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})`
114	
115	is a polynomial in `m` by Snapper's Lemma; the polynomial coefficients
116	depend on `s` through the fiber `F|_{X_s}`. When `F` is `S`-flat the
117	function `s ↦ Φ_{F,s}` is locally constant on `S`.
118	
119	iter-177+: the body unfolds to the graded-Euler-characteristic
120	construction once `χ` of a coherent sheaf on a noetherian scheme +
121	Snapper's polynomial-eventually-property are in scope. For the iter-176
122	file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
123	noncomputable def hilbertPolynomial {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
124	    (_π : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType _π] (_L _F : X.Modules) (_s : S) :
125	    Polynomial ℚ :=
126	  sorry
127	
128	/-! ## §2. The Quot functor
129	
130	The Quot functor `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` sends an `S`-scheme `T ⟶ S` to the
131	set of equivalence classes `⟨F, q⟩` of pairs `(F, q)` where
132	- `F` is a coherent sheaf on `X_T = X ×_S T` whose schematic support is
133	  proper over `T` and which is `T`-flat,
134	- `q : E_T ↠ F` is a surjective `O_{X_T}`-linear homomorphism,
135	- the fiberwise Hilbert polynomial of `F|_{X_t}` with respect to `L|_{X_t}`
136	  equals `Φ` at every `t ∈ T`.
137	
138	Two pairs `(F, q)` and `(F', q')` are equivalent iff `ker(q) = ker(q')`.
139	
140	The Hilbert scheme is the special case `E = O_X`:
141	`Hilb^{Φ,L}_{X/S} = Quot^{Φ,L}_{O_X/X/S}`.
142	
143	Blueprint reference: `def:quot_functor` (Nitsure §1; FGA Explained Ch. 5). -/
144	
145	/-- The **Quot functor** `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` of coherent quotients of `E`
146	on `X ×_S -` with Hilbert polynomial `Φ`.
147	
148	Encoded as a contravariant functor `(Over S)ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u`, sending an
149	`S`-scheme `T → S` (i.e. an object of `Over S`) to the set of
150	equivalence classes `⟨F, q⟩` of pairs `(F, q)` of a `T`-flat coherent
151	sheaf `F` on `X ×_S T` with proper support and a surjection
152	`q : E_T ↠ F` whose fiberwise Hilbert polynomial is `Φ`, modulo
153	`ker(q) = ker(q')`. Functoriality is pullback of the quotient along
154	`X ×_S T' ⟶ X ×_S T`.
155	
156	iter-177+: the body packages the on-objects / on-morphisms data using the
157	`Scheme.Modules.pullback` bifunctor on the relative product
158	`X ×_S T`, with the equivalence relation `ker(q) = ker(q')` quotiented
159	out via `Setoid` / `Quotient`. For the iter-176 file-skeleton the body
160	is a typed `sorry`. -/
161	noncomputable def QuotFunctor {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
162	    (_π : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType _π] (_L _E : X.Modules)
163	    (_Φ : Polynomial ℚ) :
164	    (Over S)ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u :=
165	  sorry
166	
167	end Scheme
168	
169	/-! ## §3. The Grassmannian scheme
170	
171	Since Mathlib carries no Grassmannian *scheme*, we encode it here as a
172	contravariant functor on `Over S` together with a representability
173	statement. The construction proceeds by gluing `binom(r, d)` affine
174	charts `U^I ≅ A^{d(r-d)}_S` along the Plücker cocycle, yielding a smooth
175	projective `S`-scheme `Gr_S(V, d)` of relative dimension `d(r-d)`,
176	equipped with a tautological rank-`d` quotient
177	`π* V ↠ U` and the Plücker closed embedding into `ℙ_S(⋀^d V)`.
178	
179	Blueprint references: `def:grassmannian_scheme`,
180	`thm:grassmannian_representable` (Nitsure §1 Exercise (2),
181	"Construction of Grassmannian"; FGA Explained Ch. 5). -/
182	
183	namespace Scheme
184	
185	/-- The **Grassmannian functor** `Grass(V, d) : (Sch/S)^op ⥤ Set` of
186	rank-`d` quotients of a locally free `O_S`-module `V` of rank `r ≥ d`.
187	
188	Encoded as the functor sending an `S`-scheme `T → S` to the set of
189	equivalence classes `⟨F, q⟩` of pairs `(F, q)` with
190	`q : V_T ↠ F` a surjection of `O_T`-modules and `F` locally free of
191	rank `d`, modulo `ker(q) = ker(q')`. Concretely
192	`Grass(V, d) = Quot^{d, O_S}_{V/S/S}` (the Quot functor for `X = S`,
193	`E = V`, constant Hilbert polynomial `d`).
194	
195	iter-177+: the body re-exports `QuotFunctor (𝟙 S) (?) V Φ_d`, where
196	`Φ_d : Polynomial ℚ` is the constant polynomial `d`. For the iter-176
197	file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
198	noncomputable def Grassmannian {S : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
199	    (_V : S.Modules) (_d : ℕ) :
200	    (Over S)ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u :=
201	  sorry
202	
203	/-- **Representability of the Grassmannian.**
204	
205	For a noetherian scheme `S`, a locally free `O_S`-module `V` of rank `r`,
206	and `1 ≤ d ≤ r`, the Grassmannian functor `Grass(V, d)` of
207	`Grassmannian` is representable by a smooth projective `S`-scheme
208	`Gr_S(V, d) ⟶ S` of relative dimension `d(r-d)`, equipped with a
209	tautological rank-`d` quotient `π* V ↠ U`. The determinant line bundle
210	`det(U)` is relatively very ample, giving a Plücker closed embedding
211	`Gr_S(V, d) ↪ ℙ_S(⋀^d V)`.
212	
213	We package the conclusion as the existence of a representing
214	`Y : Over S` together with a `Functor.RepresentableBy Y` witness for
215	`Grassmannian V d`; the additional projective / smooth / Plücker
216	structure is implicit in the construction and is iter-177+ refinement
217	work (once the proof body lands).
218	
219	iter-177+: the body follows Nitsure §1 "Construction of Grassmannian":
220	glue the `binom(r, d)` affine charts `U^I ≅ A^{d(r-d)}_S` along the
221	Plücker cocycle, verify separatedness via the diagonal cut, verify
222	properness by the DVR valuative criterion, build the tautological
223	quotient `U`, exhibit the Plücker embedding via the determinant line
224	bundle. For the iter-176 file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
225	theorem Grassmannian.representable {S : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
226	    (V : S.Modules) (d : ℕ) :
227	    ∃ (Y : Over S), Nonempty ((Grassmannian V d).RepresentableBy Y) := by
228	  sorry
229	
230	end Scheme
231	
232	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — Quot/Grassmannian predicates
233	
234	These declarations build the support/freeness predicates of
235	`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_QuotScheme.tex`, §"Support and freeness
236	predicates". Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no rank-`d` local
237	freeness predicate for sheaves of modules on a scheme, so it is built here. -/
238	
239	namespace SheafOfModules
240	
241	/-- **Locally free of rank `d`** for a sheaf of modules on a scheme.
242	
243	A sheaf of modules `M` on a scheme `X` is *locally free of rank `d`* when `X`
244	admits an open cover `{U i}` on each member of which the restriction
245	`M|_{U i}` (the pullback of `M` along the open immersion `(U i).ι`) is
246	isomorphic to the free module of rank `d`, `O_{U i}^{⊕ d}` (encoded as
247	`SheafOfModules.free (ULift (Fin d))` over the structure-ring sheaf of the
248	open subscheme `(U i).toScheme`).
249	
250	This predicate is project-local: Mathlib does not supply a rank-indexed local
251	freeness predicate for sheaves of modules on a scheme. Blueprint:
252	`def:is_locally_free_of_rank` (Nitsure §1, Exercise (2)). -/
253	def IsLocallyFreeOfRank {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) (d : ℕ) : Prop :=
254	  ∃ (ι : Type u) (U : ι → X.Opens), (⨆ i, U i = ⊤) ∧
255	    ∀ i, Nonempty ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (U i).ι).obj M ≅
256	      _root_.SheafOfModules.free (R := (U i).toScheme.ringCatSheaf) (ULift.{u} (Fin d)))
257	
258	end SheafOfModules
259	
260	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — annihilator ideal sheaf and schematic support
261	
262	These declarations build the annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules and the
263	support/properness predicates of `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_QuotScheme.tex`,
264	§"Support and freeness predicates". Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no
265	annihilator ideal sheaf for sheaves of modules on a scheme, nor a schematic-support
266	or proper-support predicate, so they are built here.
267	
268	The annihilator is packaged via `Scheme.IdealSheafData.ofIdeals`, exactly mirroring
269	Mathlib's `Scheme.Hom.ker` (which is `ofIdeals fun U ↦ RingHom.ker (f.app U).hom`):
270	`ofIdeals` produces *the largest ideal sheaf contained in* an arbitrary affine-local
271	family of ideals, so the structure's `map_ideal_basicOpen` coherence is discharged
272	internally and need not be supplied at definition time. The basic-open coherence that
273	makes the local annihilators agree with `ofIdeals` (the analogue of `Hom.ker_apply`,
274	`def:modules_annihilator`) is the separate characterization lemma `annihilator_ideal`,
275	which depends on the not-yet-closed QCoh→localization bridge
276	`isLocalizedModule_basicOpen` (`lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen`) together with
277	the algebra engine `Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map`
278	(`lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map`); see the handoff in
279	`task_results/.../QuotScheme.md`. -/
280	
281	namespace Scheme.Modules
282	
283	variable {X : Scheme.{u}}
284	
285	/-- The **annihilator ideal sheaf** of a sheaf of modules `F` on a scheme `X`
286	(`def:modules_annihilator`).
287	
288	On each affine open `U`, the intended section is the annihilator
289	`Ann_{Γ(X,U)}(Γ(F,U))` of the `Γ(X,U)`-module of sections `Γ(F,U)`. The ideal sheaf
290	is assembled with `Scheme.IdealSheafData.ofIdeals`, the largest ideal sheaf contained
291	in that affine-local family — exactly the construction used for `Scheme.Hom.ker`. This
292	sidesteps proving the basic-open coherence (`map_ideal_basicOpen`) at definition time;
293	the identity `(annihilator F).ideal U = Ann_{Γ(X,U)}(Γ(F,U))` is the downstream
294	characterization lemma (`annihilator_ideal`, blocked on the QCoh localization bridge).
295	
296	This is a project-local primitive: Mathlib does not carry an annihilator ideal sheaf
297	for sheaves of modules on a scheme. -/
298	noncomputable def annihilator (F : X.Modules) : X.IdealSheafData :=
299	  IdealSheafData.ofIdeals fun U => Module.annihilator Γ(X, U.1) Γ(F, U.1)
300	
301	/-- The component of the annihilator ideal sheaf at an affine open is contained in the
302	module annihilator of the sections. This is the always-available (`ofIdeals`) direction
303	of the characterization; the reverse inclusion is the basic-open coherence blocked on
304	`isLocalizedModule_basicOpen`. Project-local because `annihilator` is. -/
305	lemma annihilator_ideal_le (F : X.Modules) (U : X.affineOpens) :
306	    (annihilator F).ideal U ≤ Module.annihilator Γ(X, U.1) Γ(F, U.1) :=
307	  IdealSheafData.ideal_ofIdeals_le _ _
308	
309	/-- The **schematic support** of a sheaf of modules `F` on a scheme `X`
310	(`def:schematic_support`): the closed subscheme of `X` cut out by the annihilator
311	ideal sheaf `annihilator F`. Project-local because `annihilator` is. -/
312	noncomputable def schematicSupport (F : X.Modules) : Scheme.{u} :=
313	  (annihilator F).subscheme
314	
315	/-- The canonical closed immersion of the schematic support into the ambient scheme,
316	realizing `schematicSupport F` as a closed subscheme of `X` (`def:schematic_support`).
317	This is the `IdealSheafData.subschemeι` of the annihilator ideal sheaf; it is a
318	`IsPreimmersion` + `QuasiCompact` immersion onto the support. Project-local because
319	`annihilator` is. -/
320	noncomputable def schematicSupportι (F : X.Modules) : schematicSupport F ⟶ X :=
321	  (annihilator F).subschemeι
322	
323	/-- The sheaf of modules `F` **has proper support over `S` along `f`**
324	(`def:has_proper_support`): the composite of the schematic-support immersion with
325	`f : X ⟶ S` is a proper morphism. Since `AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper` is stable under
326	base change, this condition is preserved by pullback, as required by the Quot functor's
327	pullback action. Project-local because `schematicSupport` is. -/
328	def HasProperSupport {S : Scheme.{u}} (f : X ⟶ S) (F : X.Modules) : Prop :=
329	  IsProper (schematicSupportι F ≫ f)
330	
331	end Scheme.Modules
332	
333	end AlgebraicGeometry

335	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — annihilator under localization
336	
337	The annihilator ideal sheaf `def:modules_annihilator` of a coherent sheaf is
338	built from the affine-local data `U ↦ Ann_{O(U)}(F(U))`, packaged as a
339	`Scheme.IdealSheafData`. The structure's coherence field `map_ideal_basicOpen`
340	requires the algebraic fact that, for a *finitely generated* module, the
341	annihilator commutes with localization:
342	`Ann(S⁻¹M) = (Ann M)·S⁻¹R`. Mathlib (at the pinned commit) does not carry this
343	lemma, so it is supplied here as the load-bearing engine for that construction.
344	-/
345	
346	namespace Module
347	
348	/-- For a finitely generated module `M` over a commutative ring `R`, the
349	annihilator commutes with localization: if `Rₚ` localizes `R` at a submonoid
350	`S` and `f : M →ₗ[R] Mₚ` localizes `M` at `S`, then the annihilator of `Mₚ`
351	over `Rₚ` is the extension (`Ideal.map` along `algebraMap R Rₚ`) of the
352	annihilator of `M` over `R`.
353	
354	This is the abstract `IsLocalization`/`IsLocalizedModule` form, matching the
355	shape needed for the affine-basic-open coherence of the annihilator ideal sheaf
356	(`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator`, `def:modules_annihilator`):
357	the structure-sheaf restriction `Γ(X,U) → Γ(X, D(f))` is
358	`IsLocalization (powers f)`, and for a quasi-coherent `F` the section
359	restriction is `IsLocalizedModule (powers f)`.
360	
361	Mathlib has no annihilator-localization lemma, so this is project-local. -/
362	theorem annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map
363	    {R : Type*} [CommRing R] (S : Submonoid R)
364	    {Rₚ : Type*} [CommRing Rₚ] [Algebra R Rₚ] [IsLocalization S Rₚ]
365	    {M : Type*} [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] [Module.Finite R M]
366	    {Mₚ : Type*} [AddCommGroup Mₚ] [Module R Mₚ] [Module Rₚ Mₚ] [IsScalarTower R Rₚ Mₚ]
367	    (f : M →ₗ[R] Mₚ) [IsLocalizedModule S f] :
368	    Module.annihilator Rₚ Mₚ = (Module.annihilator R M).map (algebraMap R Rₚ) := by
369	  classical
370	  obtain ⟨t, htop⟩ := (Module.Finite.fg_top (R := R) (M := M))
371	  -- annihilating a spanning finset suffices for membership in the annihilator
372	  have key : ∀ (r : R), (∀ m ∈ t, r • m = 0) → r ∈ Module.annihilator R M := by
373	    intro r h
374	    rw [Module.mem_annihilator]
375	    intro x
376	    have hx : x ∈ Submodule.span R (t : Set M) := htop ▸ Submodule.mem_top
377	    induction hx using Submodule.span_induction with
378	    | mem y hy => exact h y hy
379	    | zero => simp
380	    | add a b _ _ ha hb => rw [smul_add, ha, hb, add_zero]
381	    | smul c a _ ha => rw [smul_comm, ha, smul_zero]
382	  apply le_antisymm
383	  · -- `Ann Rₚ Mₚ ⊆ (Ann R M).map`: clear one common denominator over the generators
384	    intro y hy
385	    rw [Module.mem_annihilator] at hy
386	    obtain ⟨⟨a, s⟩, rfl⟩ := IsLocalization.mk'_surjective S y
387	    dsimp only at hy ⊢
388	    have hgen : ∀ m ∈ t, ∃ u : S, (u : R) • a • m = 0 := by
389	      intro m hm
390	      have hz := hy (IsLocalizedModule.mk' f m (1 : S))
391	      rw [IsLocalizedModule.mk'_smul_mk' Rₚ f, IsLocalizedModule.mk'_eq_zero,
392	        IsLocalizedModule.eq_zero_iff S f] at hz
393	      obtain ⟨u, hu⟩ := hz
394	      exact ⟨u, hu⟩
395	    choose u hu using hgen
396	    obtain ⟨U, hU⟩ : ∃ U : S, ∀ m ∈ t, (U : R) • a • m = 0 := by
397	      refine ⟨∏ x ∈ t.attach, u x.1 x.2, ?_⟩
398	      intro m hm
399	      obtain ⟨c, hc⟩ :=
400	        Finset.dvd_prod_of_mem (fun x : t => u x.1 x.2) (Finset.mem_attach t ⟨m, hm⟩)
401	      have hcoe : ((∏ x ∈ t.attach, u x.1 x.2 : S) : R) = (u m hm : R) * (c : R) := by
402	        rw [hc]; push_cast; ring
403	      rw [hcoe, mul_smul, smul_comm, hu m hm, smul_zero]
404	    have hUa : (U : R) * a ∈ Module.annihilator R M := by
405	      apply key; intro m hm; rw [mul_smul]; exact hU m hm
406	    have heq : IsLocalization.mk' Rₚ a s
407	        = (algebraMap R Rₚ ((U : R) * a)) * IsLocalization.mk' Rₚ 1 (U * s) := by
408	      rw [← IsLocalization.mk'_eq_mul_mk'_one, IsLocalization.mk'_eq_iff_eq]
409	      push_cast; ring
410	    rw [heq]
411	    exact Ideal.mul_mem_right _ _ (Ideal.mem_map_of_mem _ hUa)
412	  · -- `(Ann R M).map ⊆ Ann Rₚ Mₚ`: the image of an annihilator annihilates
413	    rw [Ideal.map_le_iff_le_comap]
414	    intro a ha
415	    rw [Ideal.mem_comap, Module.mem_annihilator]
416	    rw [Module.mem_annihilator] at ha
417	    intro x
418	    obtain ⟨⟨m, s⟩, rfl⟩ := IsLocalizedModule.mk'_surjective S f x
419	    dsimp only [Function.uncurry]
420	    rw [← IsLocalization.mk'_one (M := S) Rₚ a, IsLocalizedModule.mk'_smul_mk' Rₚ f, ha m,
421	      IsLocalizedModule.mk'_zero]
422	
423	end Module
424	
425	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — graded Hilbert–Serre rationality -/
426	
427	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
428	
429	open PowerSeries Polynomial in
430	private lemma coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul (F : ℚ⟦X⟧) (n : ℕ) :
431	    ((PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ 1).val * F).coeff n
432	      = ∑ k ∈ Finset.range (n + 1), F.coeff k := by
433	  have h1 : (PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ 1).val = PowerSeries.mk (fun _ => (1 : ℚ)) := by
434	    have := PowerSeries.invOneSubPow_val_succ_eq_mk_add_choose (S := ℚ) (d := 0)
435	    simpa using this
436	  rw [h1, PowerSeries.coeff_mul, Finset.Nat.sum_antidiagonal_eq_sum_range_succ_mk]
437	  simp only [PowerSeries.coeff_mk, one_mul]
438	  rw [← Finset.sum_range_reflect (fun k => F.coeff k) (n + 1)]
439	  apply Finset.sum_congr rfl
440	  intro x _
441	  congr 1
442	
443	open PowerSeries Polynomial in
444	/-- **Antidifference step for rational Hilbert series.** If the first difference
445	`H (n+1) - H n` is, for `n ≫ 0`, the `n`-th coefficient of the rational series
446	`q · (1-X)^{-e}`, then `H` itself is, for `n ≫ 0`, the `n`-th coefficient of
447	`p · (1-X)^{-(e+1)}` for an explicit polynomial `p`. This is the power-series
448	heart of the inductive step in graded Hilbert–Serre (Stacks 00K1). Project-local:
449	Mathlib supplies only the converse extraction `Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly`. -/
450	private lemma rationalHilbert_antidiff
451	    (H δ : ℕ → ℚ) (q : Polynomial ℚ) (e N : ℕ)
452	    (hδ : ∀ n, N < n → δ n = ((q : ℚ⟦X⟧) * (PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ e).val).coeff n)
453	    (hH : ∀ n, N < n → H (n + 1) - H n = δ (n + 1)) :
454	    ∃ (p : Polynomial ℚ), ∀ n, N < n →
455	      H n = ((p : ℚ⟦X⟧) * (PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ (e + 1)).val).coeff n := by
456	  set F : ℚ⟦X⟧ := (q : ℚ⟦X⟧) * (PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ e).val with hF
457	  -- Partial-sum identity: the order-`(e+1)` series accumulates the order-`e` coefficients.
458	  have hsum : ∀ m, ((q : ℚ⟦X⟧) * (PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ (e + 1)).val).coeff m
459	      = ∑ k ∈ Finset.range (m + 1), F.coeff k := by
460	    intro m
461	    have hmul : (q : ℚ⟦X⟧) * (PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ (e + 1)).val
462	        = (PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ 1).val * F := by
463	      rw [hF, show (e + 1) = 1 + e from by omega, PowerSeries.invOneSubPow_add, Units.val_mul]
464	      ring
465	    rw [hmul, coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul]
466	  -- Telescoping `H` from its first differences, expressed via `F`.
467	  have hstep : ∀ n, N < n → H (n + 1) - H n = F.coeff (n + 1) := by
468	    intro n hn
469	    rw [hH n hn, hδ (n + 1) (by omega)]
470	  have htel : ∀ j, H (N + 1 + j)
471	      = H (N + 1) + ∑ i ∈ Finset.range j, F.coeff (N + 2 + i) := by
472	    intro j
473	    induction j with
474	    | zero => simp
475	    | succ j ih =>
476	        rw [Finset.sum_range_succ, show N + 2 + j = N + 1 + (j + 1) from by omega]
477	        have hs := hstep (N + 1 + j) (by omega)
478	        rw [show (N + 1 + j) + 1 = N + 1 + (j + 1) from by omega] at hs
479	        linarith [hs, ih]
480	  -- Constant-absorption: a constant function is the order-`(e+1)` coefficient of `C·(1-X)^e`.
481	  have hCconst : ∀ (c : ℚ),
482	      c • (PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ 1).val
483	        = ((Polynomial.C c * (1 - Polynomial.X) ^ e : Polynomial ℚ) : ℚ⟦X⟧)
484	            * (PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ (e + 1)).val := by
485	    intro c
486	    have hkey : (1 - PowerSeries.X : ℚ⟦X⟧) ^ e * (PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ (e + 1)).val
487	        = (PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ 1).val := by
488	      rw [Nat.add_comm e 1]
489	      exact PowerSeries.one_sub_pow_mul_invOneSubPow_val_add_eq_invOneSubPow_val ℚ 1 e
490	    rw [Polynomial.coe_mul, Polynomial.coe_C, Polynomial.coe_pow, Polynomial.coe_sub,
491	      Polynomial.coe_one, Polynomial.coe_X, mul_assoc, hkey, PowerSeries.smul_eq_C_mul]
492	  have hcoeff1 : ∀ m, (PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ 1).val.coeff m = 1 := by
493	    intro m
494	    have h1 : (PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ 1).val = PowerSeries.mk (fun _ => (1 : ℚ)) := by
495	      have := PowerSeries.invOneSubPow_val_succ_eq_mk_add_choose (S := ℚ) (d := 0)
496	      simpa using this
497	    rw [h1, PowerSeries.coeff_mk]
498	  -- Assemble the polynomial numerator.
499	  set B : ℚ := ∑ k ∈ Finset.range (N + 2), F.coeff k with hB
500	  set C0 : ℚ := H (N + 1) - B with hC0
501	  refine ⟨Polynomial.C C0 * (1 - Polynomial.X) ^ e + q, ?_⟩
502	  intro n hn
503	  -- Rewrite `H n` via the telescoping identity at `j = n - (N+1)`.
504	  obtain ⟨j, rfl⟩ : ∃ j, n = N + 1 + j := ⟨n - (N + 1), by omega⟩
505	  rw [htel j]
506	  -- The tail sum is an `Ico`-window of `F`.
507	  have htail : ∑ i ∈ Finset.range j, F.coeff (N + 2 + i)
508	      = ∑ k ∈ Finset.Ico (N + 2) (N + 2 + j), F.coeff k := by
509	    rw [Finset.sum_Ico_eq_sum_range]
510	    simp
511	  rw [htail]
512	  -- Split `range (n+1) = range (N+2) ∪ Ico (N+2) (n+1)` in the partial-sum identity.
513	  have hsplit : ∑ k ∈ Finset.range (N + 1 + j + 1), F.coeff k
514	      = B + ∑ k ∈ Finset.Ico (N + 2) (N + 2 + j), F.coeff k := by
515	    rw [hB, Finset.range_eq_Ico, Finset.range_eq_Ico,
516	      show N + 1 + j + 1 = N + 2 + j from by omega,
517	      ← Finset.sum_Ico_consecutive _ (Nat.zero_le (N + 2)) (by omega : N + 2 ≤ N + 2 + j)]
518	  -- Now compute the target coefficient and match.
519	  rw [show ((Polynomial.C C0 * (1 - Polynomial.X) ^ e + q : Polynomial ℚ) : ℚ⟦X⟧)
520	        = ((Polynomial.C C0 * (1 - Polynomial.X) ^ e : Polynomial ℚ) : ℚ⟦X⟧) + (q : ℚ⟦X⟧)
521	      from by push_cast; ring,
522	    add_mul, map_add, ← hCconst C0]
523	  rw [show (C0 • (PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ 1).val).coeff (N + 1 + j)
524	        = C0 * (PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ 1).val.coeff (N + 1 + j)
525	      from by rw [map_smul]; rfl, hcoeff1, mul_one,
526	    hsum (N + 1 + j), hsplit, hC0]
527	  ring
528	
529	open PowerSeries Polynomial in
530	/-- Internal predicate for graded Hilbert–Serre: `f : ℕ → ℚ` is, for `n ≫ 0`, the
531	`n`-th coefficient of the rational power series `p · (1-X)^{-d}` for some numerator
532	polynomial `p`. The closure lemmas below (`bump`, `sub`, `shiftRight`, `antidiff`,
533	`ofEventuallyZero`) are the inductive toolkit for the rationality proof. -/
534	private def IsRatHilb (f : ℕ → ℚ) (d : ℕ) : Prop :=
535	  ∃ (p : Polynomial ℚ) (N : ℕ), ∀ n, N < n →
536	    f n = ((p : ℚ⟦X⟧) * (PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ d).val).coeff n
537	
538	/-- An eventually-zero Hilbert function is rational of order `0` (numerator `0`). -/
539	private lemma IsRatHilb.ofEventuallyZero {f : ℕ → ℚ} (N : ℕ) (hf : ∀ n, N < n → f n = 0) :
540	    IsRatHilb f 0 := by
541	  refine ⟨0, N, fun n hn => ?_⟩
542	  rw [hf n hn]
543	  simp
544	
545	open PowerSeries Polynomial in
546	/-- The order of a rational Hilbert function may be raised by one (multiply the
547	numerator by `(1-X)`); this lets two series be brought to a common denominator. -/
548	private lemma IsRatHilb.bump {f : ℕ → ℚ} {d : ℕ} (h : IsRatHilb f d) :
549	    IsRatHilb f (d + 1) := by
550	  obtain ⟨p, N, hp⟩ := h
551	  refine ⟨p * (1 - Polynomial.X), N, fun n hn => ?_⟩
552	  rw [hp n hn]
553	  congr 1
554	  have hkey : (1 - PowerSeries.X : ℚ⟦X⟧) ^ 1 * (PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ (d + 1)).val
555	      = (PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ d).val :=
556	    PowerSeries.one_sub_pow_mul_invOneSubPow_val_add_eq_invOneSubPow_val ℚ d 1
557	  rw [pow_one] at hkey
558	  rw [Polynomial.coe_mul, Polynomial.coe_sub, Polynomial.coe_one, Polynomial.coe_X,
559	    mul_assoc, hkey]
560	
561	open PowerSeries Polynomial in
562	/-- Rational Hilbert functions of the same order are closed under pointwise difference. -/
563	private lemma IsRatHilb.sub {f g : ℕ → ℚ} {d : ℕ} (hf : IsRatHilb f d) (hg : IsRatHilb g d) :
564	    IsRatHilb (fun n => f n - g n) d := by
565	  obtain ⟨p, Np, hp⟩ := hf
566	  obtain ⟨q, Nq, hq⟩ := hg
567	  refine ⟨p - q, max Np Nq, fun n hn => ?_⟩
568	  simp only
569	  rw [hp n (lt_of_le_of_lt (le_max_left _ _) hn), hq n (lt_of_le_of_lt (le_max_right _ _) hn),
570	    Polynomial.coe_sub, sub_mul, map_sub]
571	
572	open PowerSeries Polynomial in
573	/-- Right-shift closure: if `f` is rational of order `d`, so is `n ↦ f (n-1)`
574	(multiply the numerator by `X`). -/
575	private lemma IsRatHilb.shiftRight {f : ℕ → ℚ} {d : ℕ} (h : IsRatHilb f d) :
576	    IsRatHilb (fun n => f (n - 1)) d := by
577	  obtain ⟨p, N, hp⟩ := h
578	  refine ⟨Polynomial.X * p, N + 1, fun n hn => ?_⟩
579	  obtain ⟨m, rfl⟩ : ∃ m, n = m + 1 := ⟨n - 1, by omega⟩
580	  simp only [Nat.add_sub_cancel]
581	  rw [hp m (by omega), Polynomial.coe_mul, Polynomial.coe_X, mul_assoc,
582	    PowerSeries.coeff_succ_X_mul]
583	
584	/-- The antidifference step, packaged for the predicate: if `g` is rational of order
585	`e` and `H (n+1) - H n = g (n+1)` eventually, then `H` is rational of order `e+1`. -/
586	private lemma IsRatHilb.antidiff {H g : ℕ → ℚ} {e N : ℕ} (hg : IsRatHilb g e)
587	    (hH : ∀ n, N < n → H (n + 1) - H n = g (n + 1)) : IsRatHilb H (e + 1) := by
588	  obtain ⟨q, Ng, hq⟩ := hg
589	  obtain ⟨p, hp⟩ := rationalHilbert_antidiff H g q e (max N Ng)
590	    (fun n hn => hq n (lt_of_le_of_lt (le_max_right _ _) hn))
591	    (fun n hn => hH n (lt_of_le_of_lt (le_max_left _ _) hn))
592	  exact ⟨p, max N Ng, hp⟩
593	
594	/-- **Inductive-step engine for graded Hilbert–Serre (Stacks 00K1).** The entire
595	power-series side of the inductive step: if the Hilbert function `hM` of `M` has
596	first difference matching the alternating sum `hC (n+1) - hK n` of the Hilbert
597	functions of the cokernel `C = M/xM` and kernel `K = ker(x : M → M(1))` — the
598	content of the degreewise short exact sequence `0 → K_n → M_n → M_{n+1} → C_{n+1} → 0`
599	— and both `hC, hK` are rational of order `d`, then `hM` is rational of order `d+1`.
600	The only remaining (graded-algebra) obligation in the rationality proof is to produce
601	`hK, hC` with this difference identity and apply the induction hypothesis. -/
602	private lemma IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq {hM hC hK : ℕ → ℚ} {d N : ℕ}
603	    (hC' : IsRatHilb hC d) (hK' : IsRatHilb hK d)
604	    (hdiff : ∀ n, N < n → hM (n + 1) - hM n = hC (n + 1) - hK n) :
605	    IsRatHilb hM (d + 1) := by
606	  have hg : IsRatHilb (fun n => hC n - hK (n - 1)) d := hC'.sub hK'.shiftRight
607	  refine IsRatHilb.antidiff (g := fun n => hC n - hK (n - 1)) (N := N) hg ?_
608	  intro n hn
609	  simp only [Nat.add_sub_cancel]
610	  exact hdiff n hn
611	
612	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — graded-module API for Stacks 00K1
613	
614	This namespace builds the graded-module side of the Stacks 00K1 inductive step
615	(graded Hilbert–Serre rationality). It wraps the existing Mathlib homogeneous-submodule
616	scaffold (`Submodule.IsHomogeneous`, `DirectSum.Decomposition`, `GradedRing`,
617	`QuotSMulTop`) with the induced gradings on the derived objects (homogeneous submodule,
618	quotient module, quotient ring) that Mathlib does not supply, together with the
619	degreewise rank–nullity difference identity. Blueprint: `subsec:gradedModuleApi`
620	(G1–G5, D5). -/
621	
622	namespace GradedModule
623	
624	section G1
625	
626	variable {R M ι : Type*} [Semiring R] [AddCommMonoid M] [Module R M] [DecidableEq ι]
627	variable (ℳ : ι → Submodule R M) [DirectSum.Decomposition ℳ]
628	
629	/-- **G1(a) — independence of the graded pieces of a homogeneous submodule.** The graded
630	pieces `ℳ i ⊓ p` of any submodule `p` are independent, since they refine the independent
631	grading family `ℳ` of the ambient module. Project-local: half of the induced internal
632	direct sum decomposition of a homogeneous submodule. -/
633	theorem homogeneousSubmodule_inf_iSupIndep (p : Submodule R M) :
634	    iSupIndep fun i => ℳ i ⊓ p :=
635	  ((DirectSum.Decomposition.isInternal ℳ).submodule_iSupIndep).mono fun _ => inf_le_left
636	
637	/-- **G1(b) — a homogeneous submodule is the supremum of its graded pieces.** For an
638	internally graded module `M = ⨁ ℳ i` and a homogeneous submodule `p`
639	(`Submodule.IsHomogeneous`), `p = ⨆ i, (ℳ i ⊓ p)`. Combined with
640	`homogeneousSubmodule_inf_iSupIndep` this exhibits the induced internal direct sum grading
641	`p = ⨁ i, (ℳ i ⊓ p)` that Mathlib's `HomogeneousSubmodule` scaffold does not supply; it
642	gives the graded kernel `K = ker(x : M → M(1))` its grading
643	`K_n = ker(x : M_n → M_{n+1})`.
644	
645	Stated in the ambient `M` (independence + supremum) rather than as
646	`DirectSum.IsInternal` on the subtype `↥p`: the latter triggers a runaway `isDefEq`
647	reduction of `DirectSum.coeLinearMap` over a subtype module. Project-local: the homogeneity
648	input is `Submodule.IsHomogeneous.mem_iff`. -/
649	theorem homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_inf_eq (p : Submodule R M) (hp : p.IsHomogeneous ℳ) :
650	    ⨆ i, (ℳ i ⊓ p) = p := by
651	  letI : ∀ (i : ι) (x : ℳ i), Decidable (x ≠ 0) := fun _ _ => Classical.dec _
652	  apply le_antisymm
653	  · exact iSup_le fun i => inf_le_right
654	  · intro x hx
655	    rw [← DirectSum.sum_support_decompose ℳ x]
656	    refine Submodule.sum_mem _ fun i _ => Submodule.mem_iSup_of_mem i ?_
657	    exact Submodule.mem_inf.mpr
658	      ⟨SetLike.coe_mem (DirectSum.decompose ℳ x i),
659	        (Submodule.IsHomogeneous.mem_iff ℳ hp).mp hx i⟩

663	/-- **D5 — degreewise rank–nullity difference.** For a `κ`-linear map `φ : V → W`
664	between finite-dimensional `κ`-vector spaces,
665	`dim W − dim V = dim (W ⧸ range φ) − dim (ker φ)` (integer-valued). Applied
666	degreewise with `φ = (x : M_n → M_{n+1})` this is the `hdiff` hypothesis consumed by
667	`AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq`. Pure linear algebra — no graded structure used.
668	Project-local: Mathlib has the two halves (`LinearMap.finrank_range_add_finrank_ker`,
669	`Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank`) but not this packaged difference. -/
670	theorem degreewise_finrank_diff {κ V W : Type*} [Field κ]
671	    [AddCommGroup V] [Module κ V] [FiniteDimensional κ V]
672	    [AddCommGroup W] [Module κ W] [FiniteDimensional κ W]
673	    (φ : V →ₗ[κ] W) :
674	    (Module.finrank κ W : ℤ) - Module.finrank κ V
675	      = (Module.finrank κ (W ⧸ LinearMap.range φ) : ℤ)
676	        - Module.finrank κ (LinearMap.ker φ) := by
677	  have hrn := LinearMap.finrank_range_add_finrank_ker φ
678	  have hq := Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank (LinearMap.range φ)
679	  omega

681	/-! ### Ambient subquotient induction for Stacks 00K1
682	
683	The Route-2 graded-module side of the inductive step. Everything is phrased over a
684	**fixed** ambient graded `κ`-module `M = ⨁ ℳ n`: a subquotient is a pair of homogeneous
685	submodules `N' ≤ N ⊆ M`, and its Hilbert function is the ambient dimension difference
686	`n ↦ dim_κ(N ⊓ ℳ n) − dim_κ(N' ⊓ ℳ n)`. The kernel and cokernel of a degree-one
687	endomorphism are again ambient subquotient pairs, so no `DirectSum.Decomposition` on a
688	quotient/subtype carrier is ever formed (cf.
689	`memory/graded-quotient-module-isdefeq-pathology.md`). -/
690	
691	section Subquotient
692	
693	variable {κ M : Type*} [Field κ] [AddCommGroup M] [Module κ M]
694	variable (ℳ : ℕ → Submodule κ M) [DirectSum.Decomposition ℳ]
695	
696	/-- A `κ`-linear endomorphism `x` of `M` **raises degree by one** with respect to the
697	grading `ℳ` when `x (ℳ n) ⊆ ℳ (n+1)` for every `n`. This is the abstract, graded-ring-free
698	form of "multiplication by a degree-one element" used in the Stacks 00K1 induction.
699	Project-local. -/
700	def RaisesDegree (x : M →ₗ[κ] M) : Prop := ∀ n, (ℳ n).map x ≤ ℳ (n + 1)
701	
702	omit [DirectSum.Decomposition ℳ] in
703	/-- Membership form of `RaisesDegree`. -/
704	lemma RaisesDegree.mem {x : M →ₗ[κ] M} (hx : RaisesDegree ℳ x) {n : ℕ} {m : M}
705	    (hm : m ∈ ℳ n) : x m ∈ ℳ (n + 1) :=
706	  hx n (Submodule.mem_map_of_mem hm)
707	
708	/-- The **ambient subquotient Hilbert function** of a pair of homogeneous submodules
709	`N' ≤ N ⊆ M`: `n ↦ dim_κ(N ⊓ ℳ n) − dim_κ(N' ⊓ ℳ n)` (computed in `ℤ`, cast to `ℚ`).
710	This is the data the Stacks 00K1 induction tracks; it depends only on the ambient
711	intersections `N ⊓ ℳ n`, `N' ⊓ ℳ n` of submodules of the fixed `M`. Project-local. -/
712	noncomputable def subquotientHilb (N N' : Submodule κ M) (n : ℕ) : ℚ :=
713	  (((Module.finrank κ ↥(N ⊓ ℳ n) : ℤ) - (Module.finrank κ ↥(N' ⊓ ℳ n) : ℤ) : ℤ) : ℚ)
714	
715	/-- A degree-raising endomorphism shifts the homogeneous decomposition: the degree-`(i+1)`
716	component of `x m` is `x` applied to the degree-`i` component of `m`. This is the ambient
717	commutation fact that makes preimages and images of homogeneous submodules under `x`
718	homogeneous. Project-local. -/
719	lemma decompose_raisesDegree {x : M →ₗ[κ] M} (hx : RaisesDegree ℳ x) (m : M) (i : ℕ) :
720	    (DirectSum.decompose ℳ (x m) (i + 1) : M) = x (DirectSum.decompose ℳ m i) := by
721	  classical
722	  conv_lhs => rw [← DirectSum.sum_support_decompose ℳ m, map_sum, DirectSum.decompose_sum]
723	  simp only [DirectSum.sum_apply, AddSubmonoidClass.coe_finset_sum]
724	  rw [Finset.sum_eq_single i]
725	  · exact DirectSum.decompose_of_mem_same ℳ
726	      (hx i (Submodule.mem_map_of_mem (SetLike.coe_mem _)))
727	  · intro j _ hji
728	    rw [DirectSum.decompose_of_mem_ne ℳ
729	      (hx j (Submodule.mem_map_of_mem (SetLike.coe_mem _))) (by omega : j + 1 ≠ i + 1)]
730	  · intro hi
731	    simp [DFinsupp.notMem_support_iff.mp hi]
732	
733	/-- The preimage of a homogeneous submodule under a degree-raising endomorphism is
734	homogeneous. Project-local. -/
735	lemma comap_isHomogeneous {x : M →ₗ[κ] M} (hx : RaisesDegree ℳ x)
736	    {N' : Submodule κ M} (hN' : N'.IsHomogeneous ℳ) :
737	    (N'.comap x).IsHomogeneous ℳ := by
738	  intro i z hz
739	  rw [Submodule.mem_comap, ← decompose_raisesDegree ℳ hx z i]
740	  exact (Submodule.IsHomogeneous.mem_iff ℳ hN').mp (Submodule.mem_comap.mp hz) (i + 1)
741	
742	/-- A degree-raising endomorphism kills the degree-zero component: `x m` has no degree-`0`
743	part. Project-local. -/
744	lemma decompose_raisesDegree_zero {x : M →ₗ[κ] M} (hx : RaisesDegree ℳ x) (m : M) :
745	    (DirectSum.decompose ℳ (x m) 0 : M) = 0 := by
746	  classical
747	  conv_lhs => rw [← DirectSum.sum_support_decompose ℳ m, map_sum, DirectSum.decompose_sum]
748	  simp only [DirectSum.sum_apply, AddSubmonoidClass.coe_finset_sum]
749	  refine Finset.sum_eq_zero fun j _ => ?_
750	  rw [DirectSum.decompose_of_mem_ne ℳ
751	    (hx j (Submodule.mem_map_of_mem (SetLike.coe_mem _))) (by omega : j + 1 ≠ 0)]
752	
753	/-- The image of a homogeneous submodule under a degree-raising endomorphism is
754	homogeneous. Project-local. -/
755	lemma map_isHomogeneous {x : M →ₗ[κ] M} (hx : RaisesDegree ℳ x)
756	    {N : Submodule κ M} (hN : N.IsHomogeneous ℳ) :
757	    (N.map x).IsHomogeneous ℳ := by
758	  intro i z hz
759	  obtain ⟨m, hm, rfl⟩ := hz
760	  cases i with
761	  | zero => rw [decompose_raisesDegree_zero ℳ hx m]; exact Submodule.zero_mem _
762	  | succ i =>
763	      rw [decompose_raisesDegree ℳ hx m i]
764	      exact Submodule.mem_map_of_mem ((Submodule.IsHomogeneous.mem_iff ℳ hN).mp hm i)
765	
766	/-- **Ambient image identity.** For a homogeneous submodule `N` and a degree-raising
767	endomorphism `x`, the degree-`(n+1)` part of `x · N` is `x · (N ⊓ ℳ n)`. Project-local. -/
768	lemma map_inf_degree_eq {x : M →ₗ[κ] M} (hx : RaisesDegree ℳ x)
769	    {N : Submodule κ M} (hN : N.IsHomogeneous ℳ) (n : ℕ) :
770	    N.map x ⊓ ℳ (n + 1) = (N ⊓ ℳ n).map x := by
771	  apply le_antisymm
772	  · rintro y ⟨hy1, hy2⟩
773	    obtain ⟨m, hm, rfl⟩ := hy1
774	    refine Submodule.mem_map.mpr ⟨DirectSum.decompose ℳ m n, ?_, ?_⟩
775	    · exact Submodule.mem_inf.mpr
776	        ⟨(Submodule.IsHomogeneous.mem_iff ℳ hN).mp hm n, SetLike.coe_mem _⟩
777	    · rw [← decompose_raisesDegree ℳ hx m n]
778	      exact DirectSum.decompose_of_mem_same ℳ hy2
779	  · refine le_inf (Submodule.map_mono inf_le_left) ?_
780	    rw [Submodule.map_le_iff_le_comap]
781	    exact fun m hm => hx.mem ℳ (Submodule.mem_inf.mp hm).2
782	
783	/-- **Ambient distributive law.** Intersecting a sum of two homogeneous submodules with a
784	graded piece distributes: `(P + Q) ⊓ ℳ k = (P ⊓ ℳ k) + (Q ⊓ ℳ k)`. Project-local. -/
785	lemma sup_inf_degree_eq {P Q : Submodule κ M}
786	    (hP : P.IsHomogeneous ℳ) (hQ : Q.IsHomogeneous ℳ) (k : ℕ) :
787	    (P ⊔ Q) ⊓ ℳ k = (P ⊓ ℳ k) ⊔ (Q ⊓ ℳ k) := by
788	  apply le_antisymm
789	  · rintro z ⟨hzPQ, hzk⟩
790	    obtain ⟨p, hp, q, hq, rfl⟩ := Submodule.mem_sup.mp hzPQ
791	    have hpk : (DirectSum.decompose ℳ p k : M) ∈ P ⊓ ℳ k :=
792	      Submodule.mem_inf.mpr ⟨(Submodule.IsHomogeneous.mem_iff ℳ hP).mp hp k, SetLike.coe_mem _⟩
793	    have hqk : (DirectSum.decompose ℳ q k : M) ∈ Q ⊓ ℳ k :=
794	      Submodule.mem_inf.mpr ⟨(Submodule.IsHomogeneous.mem_iff ℳ hQ).mp hq k, SetLike.coe_mem _⟩
795	    have hsum : (DirectSum.decompose ℳ p k : M) + (DirectSum.decompose ℳ q k : M) = p + q := by
796	      have h := DirectSum.decompose_of_mem_same ℳ hzk
797	      rw [DirectSum.decompose_add, DirectSum.add_apply] at h
798	      simpa using h
799	    exact hsum ▸ Submodule.add_mem_sup hpk hqk
800	  · exact sup_le (le_inf (inf_le_left.trans le_sup_left) inf_le_right)
801	      (le_inf (inf_le_left.trans le_sup_right) inf_le_right)
802	
803	/-- The intersection of two homogeneous submodules is homogeneous. Project-local: Mathlib
804	provides no lattice-closure lemmas for `Submodule.IsHomogeneous`. -/
805	lemma inf_isHomogeneous {p q : Submodule κ M} (hp : p.IsHomogeneous ℳ)
806	    (hq : q.IsHomogeneous ℳ) : (p ⊓ q).IsHomogeneous ℳ := by
807	  intro i z hz
808	  exact Submodule.mem_inf.mpr
809	    ⟨(Submodule.IsHomogeneous.mem_iff ℳ hp).mp (Submodule.mem_inf.mp hz).1 i,
810	      (Submodule.IsHomogeneous.mem_iff ℳ hq).mp (Submodule.mem_inf.mp hz).2 i⟩
811	
812	/-- The sum (supremum) of two homogeneous submodules is homogeneous. Project-local. -/
813	lemma sup_isHomogeneous {p q : Submodule κ M} (hp : p.IsHomogeneous ℳ)
814	    (hq : q.IsHomogeneous ℳ) : (p ⊔ q).IsHomogeneous ℳ := by
815	  intro i z hz
816	  obtain ⟨a, ha, b, hb, rfl⟩ := Submodule.mem_sup.mp hz
817	  have hcoe : (DirectSum.decompose ℳ (a + b) i : M)
818	      = ↑(DirectSum.decompose ℳ a i) + ↑(DirectSum.decompose ℳ b i) := by
819	    rw [DirectSum.decompose_add, DirectSum.add_apply]; rfl
820	  rw [hcoe]
821	  exact Submodule.add_mem_sup ((Submodule.IsHomogeneous.mem_iff ℳ hp).mp ha i)
822	    ((Submodule.IsHomogeneous.mem_iff ℳ hq).mp hb i)

824	/-! #### Kernel/cokernel subquotient building blocks
825	
826	For a degree-raising endomorphism `x` and a homogeneous pair `N' ≤ N`, the kernel
827	subquotient is the pair `(N ⊓ x⁻¹N', N')` and the cokernel subquotient is the pair
828	`(N, N' + x·N)`. The lemmas here record that both new pairs are homogeneous, nest correctly,
829	are annihilated by `x`, and are preserved by any endomorphism `t` commuting with `x` that
830	preserves the original pair — the ambient (carrier-free) content of
831	`lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker`. -/
832	
833	/-- The kernel subquotient's lower module `N ⊓ x⁻¹N'` is homogeneous. Project-local. -/
834	lemma ker_isHomogeneous {x : M →ₗ[κ] M} (hx : RaisesDegree ℳ x)
835	    {N N' : Submodule κ M} (hN : N.IsHomogeneous ℳ) (hN' : N'.IsHomogeneous ℳ) :
836	    (N ⊓ N'.comap x).IsHomogeneous ℳ :=
837	  inf_isHomogeneous ℳ hN (comap_isHomogeneous ℳ hx hN')
838	
839	/-- The cokernel subquotient's upper module `N' ⊔ x·N` is homogeneous. Project-local. -/
840	lemma coker_isHomogeneous {x : M →ₗ[κ] M} (hx : RaisesDegree ℳ x)
841	    {N N' : Submodule κ M} (hN : N.IsHomogeneous ℳ) (hN' : N'.IsHomogeneous ℳ) :
842	    (N' ⊔ N.map x).IsHomogeneous ℳ :=
843	  sup_isHomogeneous ℳ hN' (map_isHomogeneous ℳ hx hN)

846	/-- The kernel subquotient nests: `N' ≤ N ⊓ x⁻¹N'`, using `N' ≤ N` and that `x` preserves

848	lemma ker_le {x : M →ₗ[κ] M} {N N' : Submodule κ M} (hle : N' ≤ N)

850	  le_inf hle (Submodule.map_le_iff_le_comap.mp hpresN')

852	omit [DirectSum.Decomposition ℳ] in
853	/-- The cokernel subquotient nests: `N' ⊔ x·N ≤ N`, using `N' ≤ N` and that `x` preserves
854	`N`. Project-local. -/
855	lemma coker_le {x : M →ₗ[κ] M} {N N' : Submodule κ M} (hle : N' ≤ N)
856	    (hpresN : N.map x ≤ N) : N' ⊔ N.map x ≤ N :=
857	  sup_le hle hpresN
