665	\begin{lemma}\leanok
666	[Inductive-step engine from a degreewise short exact sequence]
667	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}
668	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq}
669	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
670	  Let \(h_M, h_C, h_K : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d, N \in \mathbb{N}\).
671	  Suppose \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\)
672	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}), and that for all \(n > N\)
673	  \[
674	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
675	  \]
676	  Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\). This packages the entire power-series
677	  side of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step: \(h_M\) is the Hilbert function of
678	  \(M\), and \(h_C, h_K\) are the Hilbert functions of the cokernel
679	  \(C = M/xM\) and kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) of multiplication by a
680	  degree-one element \(x\); the displayed identity is the alternating-sum
681	  consequence of the degreewise short exact sequence
682	  \(0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{x} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0\). Given the
683	  inductive hypothesis that \(h_C, h_K\) are rational of order \(d\), the
684	  conclusion is rationality of \(h_M\) at order \(d+1\).
685	\end{lemma}
686	
687	\begin{proof}
688	  \uses{lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
689	  Set \(g(n) = h_C(n) - h_K(n-1)\). By closure under right shift
690	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}) \(n \mapsto h_K(n-1)\) is rational of order
691	  \(d\), and by closure under difference (\cref{lem:ratHilb_sub}) so is \(g\). The
692	  hypothesis rewrites as \(h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) = g(n+1)\) for \(n > N\) (since
693	  \(g(n+1) = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)\)). Applying the antidifference step
694	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}) to \(H = h_M\) and this \(g\) gives
695	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\).
696	\end{proof}

698	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
699	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
700	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
701	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
702	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
703	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
704	  % Polynomials" (read from
705	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
706	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
707	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
708	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
709	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
710	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
711	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
712	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
713	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
714	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
715	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
716	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
717	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
718	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
719	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
720	  \[
721	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
722	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
723	  \]
724	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
725	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
726	\end{theorem}
727	
728	\begin{proof}
729	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
730	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
731	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
732	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
733	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components and
734	  degree-zero part the field \(\kappa(s)\). Applying the abstract graded
735	  Hilbert--Serre rationality lemma (\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) to the
736	  graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and the finitely generated
737	  graded module \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) exhibits the graded Hilbert
738	  function
739	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
740	  as the eventual coefficient function of a rational series: there are
741	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
742	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
743	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
744	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
745	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
746	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
747	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
748	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
749	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
750	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
751	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
752	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
753	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
754	\end{proof}

756	\subsection{The ambient subquotient induction for the Stacks~00K1 step}
757	\label{subsec:gradedModuleApi}
758	
759	The power-series side of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} is fully isolated
760	in the \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}). What remains is
761	the \emph{graded-module} side of the Stacks~00K1 induction. Rather than build the
762	kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one element as graded modules
763	over a smaller quotient ring \(R/(x)\) -- which would force a graded structure on a
764	derived carrier -- we range the induction over \emph{pairs of homogeneous
765	submodules of a single fixed ambient graded module} \(M\). A pair \((N, N')\) with
766	\(N' \le N\) homogeneous submodules of \(M\) stands for the subquotient \(N/N'\); its
767	ambient Hilbert function is the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) -
768	\dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such pairs, equipped with finitely many
769	pairwise-commuting degree-one endomorphisms preserving \(N\) and \(N'\), is
770	\emph{closed} under the kernel and cokernel of one of those endomorphisms, both
771	again presented as ambient subquotient pairs. Consequently no graded quotient ring,
772	graded quotient module, or regrading of a subtype/quotient carrier is ever formed:
773	every object that appears is an ambient family \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\)
774	of submodules of the fixed \(M\).
775	
776	This subsection records that machinery as a thin layer over existing Mathlib
777	scaffold: Mathlib already provides the notion of a homogeneous submodule
778	(\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}), the homogeneity of a span of
779	homogeneous elements (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}), the degree-shift
780	of multiplication by a homogeneous element
781	(\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), the rank--nullity identity
782	(\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}), and the finite-generation
783	transfer along a surjection (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}).
784	What is supplied here is the internal direct-sum decomposition of a homogeneous
785	submodule as an ambient family (the split \(G_1\)), the subquotient Hilbert data,
786	the kernel/cokernel closure, the degreewise difference identity, the ambient
787	finiteness transfer, and the induction itself.
788	
789	\emph{Setting for this subsection.} Let \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\) be a graded
790	ring whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a field and which is generated as a
791	\(\kappa\)-algebra in degree one, and let \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) be a graded
792	\(\kappa\)-vector space (the fixed ambient module) with finite-dimensional
793	components \(M_n\). All submodules \(N, N', \dots\) below are homogeneous submodules
794	of \(M\), and the relevant endomorphisms are \(\kappa\)-linear maps \(M \to M\)
795	raising degree by one -- typically multiplication by a homogeneous element
796	\(x \in R_1\).
797	
798	\subsubsection*{Mathlib dependency anchors}

800	\begin{lemma}[Homogeneous submodule]
801	  \label{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
802	  \lean{Submodule.IsHomogeneous}
803	  \mathlibok
804	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
805	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Submodule}).}
806	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) an
807	  internally graded \(R\)-module. A submodule \(p \subseteq M\) is
808	  \emph{homogeneous} when it is closed under taking homogeneous components:
809	  \(m \in p\) implies each graded piece \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\). Equivalently
810	  \(p = \bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n)\) as a set. Mathlib bundles this into a homogeneous
811	  submodule type providing order and extensionality structure; the ambient internal
812	  direct-sum decomposition \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\) is supplied by the split
813	  \(G_1\) (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
814	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}).
815	\end{lemma}

973	\subsubsection*{The internal grading of a homogeneous submodule (split $G_1$)}
974	
975	\begin{lemma}\leanok
976	[$G_1$ (independence) -- the family $n \mapsto M_n \cap p$ is independent]
977	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}
978	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_inf_iSupIndep}
979	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
980	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and let
981	  \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
982	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the family
983	  \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) of \(\kappa\)-subspaces of \(M\) is independent: for each
984	  \(n\),
985	  \[
986	    (M_n \cap p) \;\cap\; \Bigl(\bigsqcup_{m \ne n} (M_m \cap p)\Bigr) \;=\; 0 .
987	  \]
988	  The whole statement lives in the ambient module \(M\): the subspaces
989	  \(M_n \cap p\) are submodules of \(M\), never of a derived carrier \(\smash{\hat p}\).
990	\end{lemma}
991	
992	\begin{proof}
993	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
994	  The components \(M_n\) of the internal decomposition of \(M\) are independent. The
995	  subfamily \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) consists of submodules with \(M_n \cap p \le M_n\),
996	  so independence is inherited: a relation among the \(M_n \cap p\) is in particular a
997	  relation among the \(M_n\), forcing each term to vanish.
998	\end{proof}
999	
1000	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1001	[$G_1$ (supremum) -- $\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) = p$ for $p$ homogeneous]
1002	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
1003	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_inf_eq}
1004	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1005	  Let \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
1006	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then
1007	  \[
1008	    \bigsqcup_{n} (M_n \cap p) \;=\; p .
1009	  \]
1010	  Together with the independence of
1011	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}, this is the ambient internal
1012	  direct sum \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\), stated entirely inside \(M\) without
1013	  requiring a direct-sum decomposition of the subtype \(\smash{\hat p}\).
1014	\end{lemma}
1015	
1016	\begin{proof}
1017	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1018	  The inclusion \(\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) \le p\) is clear, since each
1019	  \(M_n \cap p \le p\). For the reverse inclusion, take \(m \in p\) and write
1020	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n\) using the
1021	  internal decomposition of \(M\). Homogeneity of \(p\) gives
1022	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\), hence \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n \cap p\); thus
1023	  \(m\) is a finite sum of elements of the \(M_n \cap p\), i.e.
1024	  \(m \in \bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p)\).
1025	\end{proof}
1026	
1027	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient class and its Hilbert function}
1028	
1029	\begin{definition}\leanok
1030	[Subquotient Hilbert data]
1031	  \label{def:graded_subquotientHilb}
1032	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotientHilb}
1033	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum}
1034	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.hilb}
1035	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1036	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
1037	  Fix a graded \(\kappa\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\), i.e.\ \(M\) together with an
1038	  internal decomposition \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) whose components
1039	  \(\mathcal{M}_n\) are finite-dimensional over \(\kappa\). A \emph{subquotient
1040	  datum of length \(r\)} consists of:
1041	  \begin{itemize}
1042	    \item a pair of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N \subseteq M\); and
1043	    \item a family \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to (M \to_\kappa M)\) of pairwise
1044	      commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms, each raising degree by one
1045	      (\(t_i(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\)), each preserving \(N\)
1046	      and \(N'\) (\(t_i N \subseteq N\), \(t_i N' \subseteq N'\)); together with
1047	    \item a finiteness condition: the subquotient \(N/N'\) is a finite module over the
1048	      polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) acting through the \(t_i\) (via the
1049	      ambient \(t\)-stable carriers \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,N\),
1050	      \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,N'\) of \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}).
1051	  \end{itemize}
1052	  The pair \((N, N')\) represents the subquotient \(N/N'\). Its \emph{ambient
1053	  Hilbert function} is
1054	  \[
1055	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \Bigl(\dim_\kappa (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
1056	      \;-\; \dim_\kappa (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\Bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Q},
1057	  \]
1058	  the difference of the (finite) \(\kappa\)-dimensions of the ambient intersections,
1059	  computed in \(\mathbb{Z}\) and cast to \(\mathbb{Q}\). Since \(N' \le N\) one has
1060	  \(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so
1061	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = \dim_\kappa\bigl((N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\bigr)\)
1062	  is the dimension of the degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\). The split \(G_1\)
1063	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
1064	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) guarantees that these ambient
1065	  intersections assemble the homogeneous submodules \(N, N'\), so the data depends
1066	  only on the ambient families \(n \mapsto N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\),
1067	  \(n \mapsto N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\).
1068	\end{definition}
1069	
1070	\subsubsection*{Ambient homogeneity calculus}
1071	
1072	The kernel/cokernel closure and the degreewise difference identity rest on a small
1073	calculus of how a degree-raising endomorphism interacts with the internal grading and
1074	with the lattice of homogeneous submodules. Throughout, \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\)
1075	is the fixed internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and \(x : M \to M\) is a
1076	\(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism raising degree by one. None of these statements forms a
1077	derived carrier: every object is a submodule of the fixed \(M\).
1078	
1079	\begin{definition}\leanok
1080	[Degree-raising endomorphism]
1081	  \label{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1082	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree}
1083	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module. A
1084	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism \(x : M \to M\) \emph{raises degree by one} when it
1085	  carries each graded piece into the next: \(x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1086	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) for every \(n\). This is the grading-ring-free form of
1087	  ``multiplication by a degree-one element'': it abstracts each of the \(r\)
1088	  commuting degree-one generators \(x \in R_1\) acting on \(M\) into a single
1089	  endomorphism, with no reference to the grading ring \(R\).
1090	\end{definition}
1091	
1092	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1093	[Membership form of degree-raising]
1094	  \label{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1095	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree.mem}
1096	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1097	  If \(x\) raises degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and \(m \in
1098	  \mathcal{M}_n\), then \(x m \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1099	\end{lemma}
1100	
1101	\begin{proof}
1102	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1103	  Immediate from the definition: \(x m \in x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1104	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1105	\end{proof}
1106	
1107	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1108	[Degree-shift commutation]
1109	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1110	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree}
1111	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1112	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then for every \(m \in M\) and every \(i\) the
1113	  degree-\((i+1)\) homogeneous component of \(x m\) equals \(x\) applied to the
1114	  degree-\(i\) component of \(m\):
1115	  \[
1116	    \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) \;=\; x\bigl(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)\bigr) .
1117	  \]
1118	  This load-bearing commutation says a degree-raising endomorphism shifts the
1119	  internal decomposition by exactly one slot; it is what makes preimages and images
1120	  of homogeneous submodules under \(x\) homogeneous.
1121	\end{lemma}
1122	
1123	\begin{proof}
1124	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1125	  Decompose \(m = \sum_j \mathrm{proj}_j(m)\) into its finitely many homogeneous
1126	  components and apply \(x\): \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with
1127	  \(x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m)) \in \mathcal{M}_{j+1}\) by
1128	  \cref{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}. Reading off the degree-\((i+1)\) component, all
1129	  terms with \(j+1 \ne i+1\) drop out, leaving \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\).
1130	\end{proof}
1131	
1132	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1133	[Degree-raising kills the bottom]
1134	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1135	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1136	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1137	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then the degree-\(0\) component of \(x m\) vanishes
1138	  for every \(m\): \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0\).
1139	\end{lemma}
1140	
1141	\begin{proof}
1142	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1143	  Writing \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with each summand in
1144	  \(\mathcal{M}_{j+1}\), no summand has degree \(0\) (every \(j+1 \ge 1\)), so the
1145	  degree-\(0\) component is \(0\).
1146	\end{proof}
1147	
1148	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1149	[Preimage of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1150	  \label{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1151	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_isHomogeneous}
1152	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1153	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N' \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
1154	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the preimage \(x^{-1}(N') =
1155	  \{m : x m \in N'\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of \(M\).
1156	\end{lemma}
1157	
1158	\begin{proof}
1159	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1160	  Let \(m \in x^{-1}(N')\) and fix a degree \(i\). By the degree-shift commutation
1161	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}), \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)) =
1162	  \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m)\), which lies in \(N'\) because \(x m \in N'\) and \(N'\)
1163	  is homogeneous. Hence \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\), so \(x^{-1}(N')\) is
1164	  homogeneous.
1165	\end{proof}
1166	
1167	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1168	[Image of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1169	  \label{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1170	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_isHomogeneous}
1171	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1172	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1173	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule.
1174	  Then the image \(x \cdot N = \{x m : m \in N\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of
1175	  \(M\), with degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(x(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) and zero
1176	  degree-\(0\) piece.
1177	\end{lemma}
1178	
1179	\begin{proof}
1180	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1181	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1182	  Take \(x m \in x \cdot N\) with \(m \in N\) and fix a degree. In degree \(0\) the
1183	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0 \in x \cdot N\)
1184	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}). In degree \(i+1\),
1185	  \(\mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\)
1186	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); since \(N\) is homogeneous,
1187	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in N\), so this component lies in \(x \cdot N\). Thus every
1188	  homogeneous component of \(x m\) lies in \(x \cdot N\), which is therefore
1189	  homogeneous.
1190	\end{proof}
1191	
1192	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1193	[Intersection of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1194	  \label{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}
1195	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.inf_isHomogeneous}
1196	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1197	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules
1198	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) then so is their intersection \(p
1199	  \cap q\). Mathlib provides no lattice-closure lemmas for homogeneous submodules, so
1200	  this is recorded here.
1201	\end{lemma}
1202	
1203	\begin{proof}
1204	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1205	  For \(m \in p \cap q\) and any degree \(i\), homogeneity of \(p\) and of \(q\)
1206	  gives \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in q\), hence
1207	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p \cap q\).
1208	\end{proof}
1209	
1210	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1211	[Sum of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1212	  \label{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1213	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_isHomogeneous}
1214	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1215	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules then so is their sum \(p + q\).
1216	\end{lemma}
1217	
1218	\begin{proof}
1219	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1220	  Write an element of \(p + q\) as \(a + b\) with \(a \in p\), \(b \in q\). For any
1221	  degree \(i\), \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) = \mathrm{proj}_i(a) + \mathrm{proj}_i(b)\)
1222	  with \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(b) \in q\) by homogeneity,
1223	  so \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) \in p + q\).
1224	\end{proof}
1225	
1226	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1227	[Ambient image identity]
1228	  \label{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}
1229	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_inf_degree_eq}
1230	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1231	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be homogeneous. Then the
1232	  degree-\((n+1)\) part of the image \(x \cdot N\) is exactly \(x\) of the
1233	  degree-\(n\) part of \(N\):
1234	  \[
1235	    (x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \;=\; x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) .
1236	  \]
1237	\end{lemma}
1238	
1239	\begin{proof}
1240	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1241	  \(\supseteq\): for \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), \(x m \in x \cdot N\) and \(x m
1242	  \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) since \(x\) raises degree. \(\subseteq\): an element \(x m
1243	  \in x \cdot N\) lying in \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) equals its own degree-\((n+1)\)
1244	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_{n+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_n(m))\)
1245	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); homogeneity of \(N\) puts
1246	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so \(x m \in x \cdot (N \cap
1247	  \mathcal{M}_n)\). The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous by
1248	  \cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}.
1249	\end{proof}
1250	
1251	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1252	[Ambient distributive law]
1253	  \label{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1254	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_inf_degree_eq}
1255	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1256	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1257	  For homogeneous submodules \(P, Q \subseteq M\) and any degree \(k\), intersecting
1258	  the sum with a graded piece distributes:
1259	  \[
1260	    (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k \;=\; (P \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k) .
1261	  \]
1262	\end{lemma}
1263	
1264	\begin{proof}
1265	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1266	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1267	  \(\supseteq\) is the general lattice inequality. For \(\subseteq\), take \(z = p +
1268	  q \in (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k\) with \(p \in P\), \(q \in Q\). Since \(z \in
1269	  \mathcal{M}_k\), \(z = \mathrm{proj}_k(z) = \mathrm{proj}_k(p) +
1270	  \mathrm{proj}_k(q)\); homogeneity of \(P\) and \(Q\)
1271	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) puts \(\mathrm{proj}_k(p) \in P \cap
1272	  \mathcal{M}_k\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_k(q) \in Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k\), so \(z \in (P
1273	  \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k)\). The intersection and sum stay in
1274	  the homogeneous lattice by \cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous} and
1275	  \cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}.
1276	\end{proof}
1277	
1278	\subsubsection*{Closure under kernel and cokernel}
1279	
1280	The kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class is realised by eight ambient
1281	component facts: for a degree-raising endomorphism \(x\) and a homogeneous pair
1282	\(N' \le N\), the kernel pair is \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel pair is
1283	\((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\). The lemmas below record that both new pairs are homogeneous,
1284	nest correctly, are annihilated by \(x\), and are preserved by any endomorphism \(t\)
1285	commuting with \(x\) that preserves the original pair.
1286	
1287	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1288	[Homogeneity of the kernel pair's lower module]
1289	  \label{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous}
1290	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_isHomogeneous}
1291	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1292	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and let \(N, N'\) be
1293	  homogeneous submodules of \(M\). Then the kernel pair's lower module
1294	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous. Indeed it is the intersection
1295	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous \(N\) with the homogeneous
1296	  preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}).
1297	\end{lemma}
1298	
1299	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1300	[Homogeneity of the cokernel pair's upper module]
1301	  \label{lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous}
1302	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_isHomogeneous}
1303	  \uses{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1304	  Under the same hypotheses, the cokernel pair's upper module \(N' + x \cdot N\) is
1305	  homogeneous: it is the sum (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous
1306	  \(N'\) with the homogeneous image \(x \cdot N\)
1307	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}).
1308	\end{lemma}
1309	
1310	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1311	[The kernel pair nests]
1312	  \label{lem:graded_ker_le}
1313	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_le}
1314	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N'\) (\(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)), then
1315	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed \(N' \le N\) by hypothesis, and \(x \cdot N'
1316	  \subseteq N'\) is exactly \(N' \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\); so \(N'\) is below the
1317	  intersection. Hence the kernel pair \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) is a valid
1318	  subquotient pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1319	\end{lemma}
1320	
1321	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1322	[The cokernel pair nests]
1323	  \label{lem:graded_coker_le}
1324	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_le}
1325	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N\) (\(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)), then
1326	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\): both summands lie in \(N\). Hence the cokernel pair
1327	  \((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) is a valid subquotient pair
1328	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1329	\end{lemma}
1330	
1331	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1332	[$x$ annihilates the kernel subquotient]
1333	  \label{lem:graded_ker_annihilate}
1334	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_annihilate}
1335	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries the kernel pair's lower module into \(N'\):
1336	  \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\). Indeed \(N \cap x^{-1}(N') \subseteq
1337	  x^{-1}(N')\), and applying \(x\) to a preimage of \(N'\) lands in \(N'\). Thus \(x\)
1338	  acts as zero on the kernel subquotient.
1339	\end{lemma}
1340	
1341	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1342	[$x$ annihilates the cokernel subquotient]
1343	  \label{lem:graded_coker_annihilate}
1344	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_annihilate}
1345	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries \(N\) into the cokernel pair's upper module:
1346	  \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\), trivially (the right summand). Thus \(x\)
1347	  acts as zero on the cokernel subquotient \(N/(N' + x \cdot N)\).
1348	\end{lemma}
1349	
1350	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1351	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the preimage]
1352	  \label{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}
1353	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_map_le_of_commute}
1354	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N'\) (\(t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)). Then
1355	  \(t\) preserves the preimage: \(t \cdot x^{-1}(N') \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed for
1356	  \(m\) with \(x m \in N'\) one has \(x(t m) = t(x m) \in t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\), so
1357	  \(t m \in x^{-1}(N')\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the kernel
1358	  pair's lower module \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\).
1359	\end{lemma}
1360	
1361	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1362	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the image]
1363	  \label{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}
1364	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_map_le_of_commute}
1365	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N\) (\(t \cdot N \subseteq N\)). Then
1366	  \(t\) preserves the image: \(t \cdot (x \cdot N) \subseteq x \cdot N\). Indeed for
1367	  \(m \in N\) one has \(t(x m) = x(t m)\) with \(t m \in N\), so \(t(x m) \in x \cdot
1368	  N\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the cokernel pair's upper
1369	  module \(N' + x \cdot N\).
1370	\end{lemma}
1371	
1372	\begin{lemma}[Kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class]
1373	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
1374	  % This is a narrative grouping lemma: its content is realised in Lean by the eight
1375	  % ambient component facts cited in \uses{} below (no single Lean declaration),
1376	  % together with the SubquotientDatum.ker/.coker constructors that bundle them into a
1377	  % length-(r-1) datum.
1378	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1379	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1380	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1381	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1382	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1383	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1384	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1385	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1386	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1387	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1388	  endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and set \(x = t_{r-1}\). Form the two new
1389	  pairs of homogeneous submodules of \(M\)
1390	  \[
1391	    K \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr),
1392	    \qquad
1393	    C \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr),
1394	  \]
1395	  where \(x^{-1}(N') = \{\,m \in M : x \cdot m \in N'\,\}\) is the preimage and
1396	  \(x \cdot N\) is the image of \(N\) under \(x\). Then:
1397	  \begin{enumerate}
1398	    \item \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N\) are homogeneous submodules of
1399	      \(M\), with \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\), so both
1400	      \(K\) and \(C\) are subquotient pairs;
1401	    \item the remaining endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\) pairwise commute,
1402	      raise degree by one, and preserve both members of \(K\) and of \(C\); and
1403	    \item \(x\) annihilates both subquotients: \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\)
1404	      and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\).
1405	  \end{enumerate}
1406	  Consequently \(K\) and \(C\) are again subquotient data, now of length \(r-1\)
1407	  (the endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\)), and \(K = \ker x\) and \(C =
1408	  \operatorname{coker} x\) on the subquotient \(N/N'\): \(K\) represents
1409	  \(\ker\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\) and \(C\) represents
1410	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\). No quotient or subtype
1411	  carrier is constructed; \(K\) and \(C\) are ambient pairs of homogeneous
1412	  submodules of the fixed \(M\).
1413	\end{lemma}
1414	
1415	\begin{proof}
1416	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1417	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1418	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1419	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1420	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1421	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1422	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1423	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1424	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1425	  \emph{Homogeneity.} The preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous: if
1426	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(x \cdot m \in N'\), then since \(x\) raises
1427	  degree by one (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) the components
1428	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) lie in distinct degrees, and
1429	  homogeneity of \(N'\) (split \(G_1\),
1430	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) forces each
1431	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N'\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\).
1432	  Thus \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is an intersection of homogeneous submodules, hence
1433	  homogeneous. The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous with degree-\((n+1)\) piece
1434	  \(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) by
1435	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}, and a sum of homogeneous
1436	  submodules is homogeneous (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib} applied at the
1437	  module level), so \(N' + x \cdot N\) is homogeneous. The inclusions
1438	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (as \(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)) and
1439	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (as \(N' \le N\) and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)) are
1440	  immediate. In the language of the ambient homogeneity calculus, this paragraph is
1441	  exactly: \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}),
1442	  \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}), and the
1443	  homogeneous lattice is closed under intersection
1444	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) and sum
1445	  (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}).
1446	
1447	  \emph{Preservation.} Each \(t_i\) with \(i \le r-2\) commutes with \(x\) and
1448	  preserves \(N, N'\); hence it preserves \(x^{-1}(N')\) (if \(x t_i m = t_i x m \in
1449	  N'\) whenever \(x m \in N'\)), so it preserves \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\), and it
1450	  preserves \(x \cdot N\) (as \(t_i x N = x t_i N \subseteq x N\)), so it preserves
1451	  \(N' + x \cdot N\). Degree-raising and commutativity are inherited.
1452	
1453	  \emph{Annihilation by \(x\).} For \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) one has
1454	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\) by definition, so \(x\) carries the first member of \(K\) into
1455	  its second; and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\) trivially. Thus \(x\) acts as
1456	  zero on \(N/N'\) restricted to \(K\) and on the cokernel quotient \(C\).
1457	
1458	  \emph{Identification with \(\ker x\) and \(\operatorname{coker} x\).} On the
1459	  subquotient \(N/N'\), the class of \(m \in N\) is killed by \(x\) iff
1460	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\), i.e.\ iff \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\); so \(\ker(x : N/N' \to
1461	  N/N')\) is exactly \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\), represented by \(K\). The image of
1462	  \(x\) on \(N/N'\) is \((N' + x \cdot N)/N'\), so the cokernel
1463	  \((N/N')/\operatorname{im} x = N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) is represented by \(C\).
1464	\end{proof}
1465	
1466	\subsubsection*{The subquotient degreewise difference identity ($D_6$)}
1467	
1468	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1469	[$D_6$ -- subquotient degreewise difference]
1470	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1471	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1472	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1473	    lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1474	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1475	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1476	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1477	  % NOTE: The proof factors the two kernel-dimension computations through a
1478	  %   helper that identifies the dimension of the preimage of a submodule under the
1479	  %   inclusion of an ambient submodule with the dimension of their ambient intersection.
1480	  %   This helper is an implementation detail and carries no public blueprint node.
1481	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
1482	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
1483	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1484	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (degreewise short exact sequence).}
1485	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1486	  Hilbert function \(h_M\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Let \(h_K\) and \(h_C\) be the
1487	  Hilbert functions of the kernel and cokernel subquotients
1488	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) of
1489	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}. Then for every \(n\)
1490	  \[
1491	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1492	  \]
1493	  Writing \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) for the
1494	  degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\) (so \(\dim_\kappa Q_n = h_M(n)\)), multiplication by
1495	  \(x\) is a \(\kappa\)-linear map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) with
1496	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\) and
1497	  \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x\) the degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\); the identity is
1498	  then the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1499	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}). Specialising to \((N, N') = (\top,
1500	  \bot)\) recovers the classical \(0 \to \mathcal{M}_n \xrightarrow{x}
1501	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x\mathcal{M}_n \to 0\).
1502	\end{lemma}
1503	
1504	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
1505	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1506	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
1507	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
1508	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
1509	%  following short exact sequence
1510	%  $$
1511	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
1512	%  $$
1513	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
1514	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
1515	\begin{proof}
1516	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
1517	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1518	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1519	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1520	  Since \(N' \le N\) are homogeneous, the split \(G_1\)
1521	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) identifies the degree-\(n\) piece
1522	  of \(N/N'\) with \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\), a
1523	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space of dimension \(h_M(n)\). Because \(x\)
1524	  raises degree by one and preserves \(N\) and \(N'\)
1525	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), it induces a \(\kappa\)-linear
1526	  map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\).
1527	
1528	  \emph{Kernel.} A class \([m] \in Q_n\) (with \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)) lies in
1529	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) iff \(x \cdot m \in N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\), i.e.\
1530	  iff \(m \in (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\). Hence \(\ker(x : Q_n \to
1531	  Q_{n+1}) = \bigl((N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\bigr)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1532	  is exactly the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\), of dimension \(h_K(n)\)
1533	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
1534	
1535	  \emph{Cokernel.} The image of \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) is
1536	  \(\bigl(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) + (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\bigr)/(N' \cap
1537	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\). Using the ambient identity \((N' + x \cdot N) \cap
1538	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}) + x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1539	  -- which is the ambient distributive law (\cref{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq})
1540	  applied to \(P = N'\), \(Q = x \cdot N\), combined with the ambient image identity
1541	  \((x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1542	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}) -- the cokernel \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x = (N \cap
1543	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})/\bigl((N' + x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\bigr)\) is the
1544	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\), of dimension \(h_C(n+1)\).
1545	
1546	  \emph{Difference.} Apply the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1547	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) to \(\varphi = (x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\),
1548	  with \(V = Q_n\), \(W = Q_{n+1}\):
1549	  \[
1550	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n)
1551	      = \dim_\kappa Q_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa Q_n
1552	      = \dim_\kappa(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
1553	      = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1554	  \]
1555	  This is the \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) hypothesis consumed by \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}.
1556	\end{proof}

1558	\subsubsection*{The free polynomial-ring module structure}
1559	
1560	The finiteness condition of a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) and
1561	its transfer down one generator (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) are
1562	phrased over the \emph{free} polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] =
1563	\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0, \dots, r-1\}, \kappa)\). The following blocks build the
1564	action of that ring on the ambient module \(M\) from the \(r\) pairwise-commuting
1565	degree-raising endomorphisms \(t_i\), keeping every carrier an ambient submodule of
1566	\(M\). The free polynomial ring -- rather than the subalgebra
1567	\(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\)
1568	-- is used precisely so the inductive transfer has the ring surjection
1569	\(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\) available
1570	(\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).
1571	
1572	\begin{definition}\leanok
1573	[Polynomial evaluation ring homomorphism]
1574	  \label{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1575	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom}
1576	  \uses{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
1577	  Let \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) be a family of
1578	  pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms. The \emph{polynomial evaluation
1579	  ring homomorphism}
1580	  \[
1581	    \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t) : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \longrightarrow
1582	      \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)
1583	  \]
1584	  sends each variable \(X_i\) to \(t_i\). It is constructed by evaluating into the
1585	  \emph{commutative} \(\kappa\)-subalgebra \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa(\{t_i\})
1586	  \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) -- commutative by
1587	  \cref{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib} because the generators commute -- and then
1588	  including \(A \hookrightarrow \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); evaluation into the
1589	  noncommutative \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) directly is not an algebra
1590	  homomorphism, which is why the commutative \(A\) is interposed.
1591	\end{definition}
1592	
1593	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1594	[Evaluation sends $X_i$ to $t_i$]
1595	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}
1596	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_X}
1597	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1598	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(X_i) = t_i\) for each \(i\). Immediate from the construction
1599	  (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}), as evaluation sends \(X_i\) to the chosen image
1600	  \(t_i\).
1601	\end{lemma}
1602	
1603	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1604	[Evaluation sends a constant to a scalar]
1605	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1606	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_C}
1607	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1608	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(C\,c) = c \cdot \mathrm{id}_M\) for each \(c \in \kappa\):
1609	  the evaluation homomorphism (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}) is \(\kappa\)-linear and
1610	  carries the constant polynomial \(C\,c\) to the scalar endomorphism \(c \cdot 1\).
1611	\end{lemma}
1612	
1613	\begin{definition}\leanok
1614	[Polynomial-ring module structure on $M$]
1615	  \label{def:graded_polyModule}
1616	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule}
1617	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1618	  The \emph{polynomial-module structure} is the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-module
1619	  structure on \(M\) obtained by restricting scalars along
1620	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)\) (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}): a polynomial \(p\) acts on
1621	  \(m \in M\) by \(p \cdot m = \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(p)(m)\). This is the module over the
1622	  free polynomial ring on which the ambient subquotient induction's finiteness condition
1623	  is measured.
1624	\end{definition}
1625	
1626	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1627	[$X_i$ acts as $t_i$]
1628	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul}
1629	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_X_smul}
1630	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}
1631	  In the polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}), \(X_i \cdot m =
1632	  t_i(m)\) for all \(m \in M\). Immediate from
1633	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(X_i) = t_i\) (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}).
1634	\end{lemma}
1635	
1636	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1637	[A constant acts as a scalar]
1638	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}
1639	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_C_smul}
1640	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1641	  In the polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}), \((C\,c) \cdot m =
1642	  c \cdot m\) for all \(c \in \kappa\), \(m \in M\). Immediate from
1643	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(C\,c) = c \cdot \mathrm{id}_M\)
1644	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}).
1645	\end{lemma}
1646	
1647	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1648	[$\kappa$-scalar tower]
1649	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_isScalarTower}
1650	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_isScalarTower}
1651	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1652	  The polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}) is compatible with the
1653	  original \(\kappa\)-action: \(\mathrm{IsScalarTower}\,\kappa\,\kappa[t_0, \dots,
1654	  t_{r-1}]\,M\). Indeed the algebra map \(\kappa \to \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)
1655	  followed by the polynomial action recovers the \(\kappa\)-action, since a scalar
1656	  \(c \in \kappa\) maps to the constant \(C\,c\), which acts as \(c\)
1657	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}).
1658	\end{lemma}
1659	
1660	\begin{definition}\leanok
1661	[Polynomial-ring submodule from a $t$-stable submodule]
1662	  \label{def:graded_polySubmodule}
1663	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polySubmodule}
1664	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul,
1665	    lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}
1666	  Let \(N \subseteq M\) be a \(\kappa\)-submodule that is stable under each \(t_i\)
1667	  (\(t_i \cdot N \subseteq N\)). Then \(N\), with the \emph{same} ambient carrier, is a
1668	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-submodule of \(M\) for the polynomial-module structure
1669	  (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}). Stability under the action of an arbitrary polynomial
1670	  follows by induction on monomials: constants act as scalars
1671	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}) and multiplication by \(X_i\) acts as \(t_i\)
1672	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul}), both of which preserve \(N\).
1673	\end{definition}
1674	
1675	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1676	[The polynomial submodule has the same carrier]
1677	  \label{lem:graded_polySubmodule_coe}
1678	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polySubmodule_coe}
1679	  \uses{def:graded_polySubmodule}
1680	  The underlying set of \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}(t, N)\)
1681	  (\cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}) equals \(N\): the lift to a
1682	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-submodule does not change the carrier, so every object
1683	  in the induction remains an ambient submodule of the fixed \(M\).
1684	\end{lemma}

1686	\subsubsection*{Finiteness-transfer infrastructure}
1687	
1688	The ambient finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer})
1689	factors the polynomial action of the larger ring through a surjection onto the
1690	smaller one. The following blocks build that surjection and the semilinearity
1691	identity it rests on, and record the two elementary finiteness manipulations
1692	(shrinking a numerator, enlarging a denominator) used to feed the kernel and
1693	cokernel constructors.
1694	
1695	\begin{definition}
1696	
1697	\leanok
1698	[The last-variable surjection of polynomial rings]
1699	  \label{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}
1700	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom}
1701	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_X_castSucc}
1702	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_X_last}
1703	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_C}
1704	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_rename_castSucc}
1705	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_surjective}
1706	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_ringHomSurjective}
1707	  \uses{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
1708	  For a field \(\kappa\) and \(r \in \mathbb{N}\), the \emph{last-variable
1709	  homomorphism} is the \(\kappa\)-algebra map
1710	  \[
1711	    \mathrm{lastVarAlgHom} : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]
1712	      = \operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0,\dots,r\}, \kappa)
1713	      \;\longrightarrow\;
1714	      \operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0,\dots,r-1\}, \kappa) = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]
1715	  \]
1716	  obtained by evaluation: it sends the last variable \(X_r\) to \(0\) and each
1717	  earlier variable \(X_i\) (for \(0 \le i < r\)) to \(X_i\), and fixes constants.
1718	  It is a left inverse of the algebra map
1719	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \to \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)
1720	  induced by the canonical inclusion \(\{0, \dots, r-1\} \hookrightarrow \{0, \dots, r\}\)

1821	\subsubsection*{Ambient finiteness transfer}
1822	
1823	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1824	[Abstract single-pair finiteness transfer down one variable]
1825	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}
1826	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_finite_transfer}
1827	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1828	    lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem,
1829	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib,
1830	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
1831	  Let \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) be pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms of
1832	  \(M\), and let \(P' \le P\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules of \(M\), each stable under
1833	  every \(t_i\). Suppose the last endomorphism \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into \(P'\)
1834	  (\(x \cdot P \subseteq P'\)), and that the subquotient \(P/P'\) is finite over the
1835	  free polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\) for the polynomial-module structure
1836	  of \cref{def:graded_polyModule} (with carriers the ambient \(t\)-stable submodules
1837	  \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\), \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\),
1838	  \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). Then \(P/P'\) is finite over
1839	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) for the reduced family \((t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})\).
1840	
1841	  This is the abstract single-pair transfer: it does not mention the kernel/cokernel
1842	  pairs, but is the engine applied to each of them by the kernel and cokernel
1843	  constructors (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker},
1844	  \cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}). Because the last variable
1845	  annihilates the subquotient, the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-action factors through
1846	  the last-variable surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r] \twoheadrightarrow
1847	  \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), which is what makes the transfer drop one variable.
1848	\end{lemma}
1849	
1850	\begin{proof}
1851	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1852	    lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem,
1853	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}
1854	  Write \(\sigma = \mathrm{lastVarAlgHom} : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]
1855	  \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) for the surjective ring
1856	  homomorphism sending the last variable to \(0\) and each earlier variable \(X_i\)
1857	  (\(i < r\)) to \(X_i\) (\cref{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}). Consider the map on numerators
1858	  \[
1859	    g : \mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P \;\longrightarrow\;
1860	      \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/
1861	        \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr), \qquad y \longmapsto [\,y\,],
1862	  \]
1863	  where the source carries the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-module structure and the
1864	  target the reduced \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-module structure (both with the
1865	  same ambient carrier \(P\), \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). The mod-\(P'\)
1866	  semilinearity identity (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem}) -- acting by
1867	  \(s\) through the full family and acting by \(\sigma(s)\) through the reduced family
1868	  agree modulo \(P'\), precisely because \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into \(P'\) --
1869	  says exactly that \(g\) is \(\sigma\)-semilinear. The map \(g\) is surjective (it is
1870	  the quotient projection on the common carrier), and it kills the denominator
1871	  \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\), so it descends through that submodule
1872	  (\cref{lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}) to a surjective
1873	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-linear map
1874	  \[
1875	    \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/\bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr)
1876	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\;
1877	    \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/\bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr)
1878	  \]
1879	  from the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finite subquotient onto the reduced one.
1880	  Finiteness transfers along this surjection
1881	  (\cref{lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}), giving finiteness of \(P/P'\)
1882	  over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\).
1883	
1884	  \emph{Why the free polynomial ring.} The smaller ring must be the \emph{free}
1885	  polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), not the subalgebra
1886	  \(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq
1887	  \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\): relations among the endomorphisms inside
1888	  \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) would obstruct the polynomial-ring surjection
1889	  \(\sigma\) that the scalar transfer requires
1890	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}). Keeping free polynomial
1891	  rings at every step keeps \(\sigma\) -- and hence the transfer -- available
1892	  throughout. Everything is finite generation of ambient submodules of the fixed
1893	  \(M\) and their quotients.
1894	\end{proof}

1999	\subsubsection*{The degreewise difference identity}
2000	
2001	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2002	[$D_5$ -- degreewise rank--nullity difference]
2003	  \label{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
2004	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.degreewise_finrank_diff}
2005	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
2006	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
2007	  Let \(\varphi : V \to W\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map between finite-dimensional
2008	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. Then
2009	  \[
2010	    \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V
2011	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi).
2012	  \]
2013	  Applied degreewise with \(\varphi = (x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\),
2014	  \(V = \mathcal{M}_n\), \(W = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi\) is the
2015	  degree-\(n\) piece \(K_n\) of the kernel subquotient and
2016	  \(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x \mathcal{M}_n\) the
2017	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(C_{n+1}\) of the cokernel subquotient
2018	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, via the split \(G_1\)
2019	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq} and the degree shift
2020	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) -- this gives, for every \(n\),
2021	  \[
2022	    \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_n
2023	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n,
2024	  \]
2025	  which is the difference identity \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) consumed by
2026	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}. No graded structure is used in proving the
2027	  underlying vector-space identity -- it is pure rank--nullity.
2028	\end{lemma}
2029	
2030	\begin{proof}
2031	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
2032	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
2033	  By rank--nullity (\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}),
2034	  \(\dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}
2035	  \varphi)\), so \(\dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa V -
2036	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). By additivity on the short exact sequence
2037	  \(0 \to \operatorname{range}\varphi \to W \to W/\operatorname{range}\varphi \to 0\)
2038	  (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}),
2039	  \(\dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W -
2040	  \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V +
2041	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). Rearranging gives
2042	  \(\dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) -
2043	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). The degreewise application identifies
2044	  \(\ker(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\) and
2045	  \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}/\operatorname{range}(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\)
2046	  with the degree-\(n\) piece of the kernel subquotient and the degree-\((n+1)\)
2047	  piece of the cokernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
2048	  the latter because the degree-\((n+1)\) graded piece of the homogeneous submodule
2049	  \(x\mathcal{M}\) is exactly \(x \mathcal{M}_n\)
2050	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}).
2051	\end{proof}

2059	  Let \(Q\) be a \(\kappa\)-vector space that is also a module over
2060	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa)\) for an \emph{empty} index set
2061	  \(\sigma\) -- in particular \(\sigma = \{0, \dots, r-1\}\) with \(r = 0\) -- in a way
2062	  compatible with the \(\kappa\)-action (scalar tower). If \(Q\) is module-finite over
2063	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa)\), then \(Q\) is finite-dimensional over
2064	  \(\kappa\). Indeed for empty \(\sigma\) the polynomial ring is
2065	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa) \cong \kappa\), a finite \(\kappa\)-module,
2066	  so finiteness of \(Q\) over it transfers to finiteness over \(\kappa\) along the scalar
2067	  tower. This is the length-zero input to the ambient subquotient induction
2068	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}): when \(r = 0\) the subquotient \(N/N'\) is a
2069	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space, hence \(\mathrm{hilb}\) is eventually zero.
2070	\end{lemma}
2071	
2072	\begin{lemma}
2073	
2074	\leanok
2075	[Base case: the length-zero subquotient Hilbert function vanishes eventually]
2076	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}
2077	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}
2078	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb,
2079	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite,
2080	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
2081	    lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
2082	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
2083	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
2084	  % 00K1), proof, base case (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex,
2085	  % L13908-L13910).
2086	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2087	  % "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
2088	  %  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
2089	  %  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds."
2090	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (base case of the induction).}
2091	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(0\)
2092	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}): a homogeneous pair \(N' \le N\) of the fixed
2093	  ambient graded module \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) with \emph{no} endomorphisms,
2094	  whose finiteness witness says the subquotient \(Q_0 := N/N'\) is module-finite over
2095	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\varnothing, \kappa)\). Then the ambient Hilbert
2096	  function vanishes eventually: there is \(K \in \mathbb{N}\) with
2097	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for all \(n > K\). Consequently
2098	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\) (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}),
2099	  which is the base case of the ambient subquotient induction
2100	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}).
2101	\end{lemma}
2102	
2103	\begin{proof}
2104	  \uses{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite,
2105	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
2106	    lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
2107	  Since the index set of variables is empty,
2108	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\varnothing, \kappa) \cong \kappa\), so the finiteness
2109	  witness makes the subquotient \(Q_0 = N/N'\) a \emph{finite-dimensional}
2110	  \(\kappa\)-vector space
2111	  (\cref{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}). For each
2112	  degree \(n\), composing the inclusion of the homogeneous piece
2113	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \hookrightarrow N\) with the quotient projection
2114	  \(N \twoheadrightarrow Q_0\) gives a \(\kappa\)-linear map
2115	  \(\psi_n : N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \to Q_0\), \(m \mapsto [m]\), whose image is the
2116	  class of the degree-\(n\) homogeneous piece inside the subquotient.
2117	
2118	  \emph{Step 1: independence of the images.} The family
2119	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\bigr)_{n}\) is \(\mathrm{iSupIndep}\) in
2120	  \(Q_0\). The reason is the ambient direct-sum grading of \(M\): the homogeneous
2121	  pieces \(\mathcal{M}_n\) form an independent family
2122	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}). An element of
2123	  \(\bigsqcup_{j \ne n} \operatorname{range}(\psi_j)\) is a finite sum of classes of
2124	  homogeneous elements of degrees \(j \ne n\); since \(N'\) is homogeneous, the
2125	  degree-\(n\) homogeneous component of any representative of such a class lies in
2126	  \(N'\), so the class has zero degree-\(n\) component in \(Q_0\). A nonzero class in
2127	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\), by contrast, is represented by a homogeneous
2128	  element of degree \(n\) not in \(N'\), so has nonzero degree-\(n\) component.
2129	  Comparing degree-\(n\) components forces the intersection
2130	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n) \cap \bigsqcup_{j \ne n} \operatorname{range}(\psi_j)
2131	  = 0\); independence follows.
2132	  % NOTE: ROUTE (b). Prove the iSupIndep by direct membership analysis in
2133	  %   ⨆_{j ≠ n} range(ψ j), reading off the degree-n homogeneous component
2134	  %   directly inside Q₀'s fixed κ-module structure.
2135	  %   No outgoing linear map out of Q₀ is constructed, so no scalar ring other than κ
2136	  %   is ever introduced.
2137	  % NOTE: ROUTE (a). Building a κ-linear detector
2138	  %   Φ : Q₀ → M/N', [m] ↦ [π_n m] (degree-n projection) via descent through the
2139	  %   subquotient fails: the descended map carries the scalar action of Q₀'s base
2140	  %   ring, but the target M/N' carries only a κ-module structure -- a scalar-ring
2141	  %   incompatibility. Route (b) avoids this by working entirely within Q₀.
2142	
2143	  \emph{Step 2: finiteness of the support.} Because \(Q_0\) is finite-dimensional and
2144	  the ranges \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\) are independent, only finitely many of
2145	  them are nonzero: the set \(\{\,n : \operatorname{range}(\psi_n) \ne 0\,\}\) is
2146	  finite (\cref{lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib}). In particular it is bounded
2147	  above, by some \(K \in \mathbb{N}\).
2148	
2149	  \emph{Step 3: eventual vanishing.} For \(n > K\) we have
2150	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n) = 0\), i.e.\ every homogeneous element of
2151	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\) has class \(0\) in \(Q_0 = N/N'\); equivalently
2152	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N'\), hence \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n = N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)
2153	  and
2154	  \[
2155	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
2156	      - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n) \;=\; 0 .
2157	  \]
2158	  Thus \(\mathrm{hilb}\) is eventually zero, and
2159	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
2160	\end{proof}

2162	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient induction}
2163	
2164	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2165	[Rationality of the ambient subquotient Hilbert function]
2166	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
2167	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_hilbertSeries_rational}
2168	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2169	    def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker, def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker,
2170	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
2171	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
2172	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
2173	    lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero,
2174	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2175	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
2176	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
2177	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
2178	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (induction on the number of
2179	  degree-one generators).}
2180	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(r\)
2181	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with ambient Hilbert function
2182	  \(\mathrm{hilb}\). Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\)
2183	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}): the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
2184	  - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), the coefficient
2185	  sequence of a rational series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-r}\) with \(p \in
2186	  \mathbb{Q}[X]\). The induction is on the length \(r\), and every object that
2187	  appears is an ambient pair of homogeneous submodules of the fixed graded module
2188	  \(M\).
2189	\end{lemma}
2190	
2191	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, induction set-up (read
2192	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
2193	% "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
2194	%  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
2195	%  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds.
2196	%  To prove the induction step, let $x\in S_1$
2197	%  be one of a minimal set of generators, such that
2198	%  the induction hypothesis applies to the
2199	%  graded ring $S/(x)$."
2200	\begin{proof}
2201	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2202	    def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker, def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker,
2203	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
2204	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
2205	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
2206	    lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero,
2207	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2208	  We induct on the length \(r\).
2209	
2210	  \emph{Base case \(r = 0\).} A length-zero datum has a finiteness witness making the
2211	  subquotient \(N/N'\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space
2212	  (\cref{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}); its degreewise
2213	  images form an independent family inside that finite-dimensional space, so only
2214	  finitely many are nonzero and \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for \(n \gg 0\)
2215	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}). By
2216	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} an eventually-zero function is rational of order
2217	  \(0\), giving \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
2218	
2219	  \emph{Inductive step \(r \ge 1\).} Set \(x = t_{r-1}\). By the kernel/cokernel
2220	  closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) the kernel subquotient
2221	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel subquotient
2222	  \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) are subquotient data of length \(r-1\), assembled by
2223	  the kernel and cokernel constructors (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker},
2224	  \cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}) whose finiteness witnesses come from the
2225	  abstract finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}). The
2226	  induction hypothesis applied
2227	  to \(K\) and \(C\) gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and
2228	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}). By the degreewise
2229	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
2230	  \[
2231	    \mathrm{hilb}(n+1) - \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)
2232	    \qquad \text{for all } n .
2233	  \]
2234	  Feeding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\), \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and this
2235	  difference identity into the inductive-step engine
2236	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}) yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\). (The
2237	  common order \(r-1\) for \(h_C\) and \(h_K\) is arranged, if necessary, by raising
2238	  the lower one with \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump}.) This completes the induction.
2239	\end{proof}
