2241	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
2242	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
2243	
2244	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
2245	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
2246	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
2247	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
2248	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
2249	
2250	\begin{definition}
2251	
2252	\leanok
2253	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
2254	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
2255	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
2256	  \uses{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
2257	  % NOTE: The definition itself is formalized and axiom-clean; the inclusion direction
2258	  % \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U) \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\)
2259	  % (\cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}) is closed. The reverse inclusion, which identifies
2260	  % the annihilator sheaf with the module-annihilator on affine opens, remains bridge-gated on
2261	  % \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
2262	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
2263	  % Polynomials" (read from
2264	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
2265	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2266	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
2267	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
2268	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
2269	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
2270	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2271	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
2272	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
2273	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
2274	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
2275	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
2276	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
2277	  \(U \subseteq X\),
2278	  \[
2279	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2280	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
2281	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
2282	  \]
2283	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
2284	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
2285	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
2286	
2287	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
2288	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
2289	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
2290	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
2291	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
2292	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
2293	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
2294	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
2295	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
2296	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
2297	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
2298	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2299	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
2300	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
2301	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
2302	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2303	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
2304	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
2305	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
2306	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
2307	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
2308	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
2309	
2310	  \emph{Note.} Mathlib does not carry an annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
2311	\end{definition}
2312	
2313	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2314	[Annihilator ideal bounded by the module annihilator on affine opens]
2315	  \label{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}
2316	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator_ideal_le}
2317	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator}
2318	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and
2319	  \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then the section over \(U\) of the annihilator
2320	  ideal sheaf \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}) is
2321	  contained in the module-annihilator of the sections of \(\mathcal{F}\) over
2322	  \(U\):
2323	  \[
2324	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2325	    \;\subseteq\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr).
2326	  \]
2327	  This is the inclusion direction available directly from the definition of
2328	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\); the reverse inclusion is the basic-open coherence
2329	  statement gated on \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}. Proved directly
2330	  in Lean. It supports the well-definedness of the schematic support
2331	  (\cref{def:schematic_support}).
2332	\end{lemma}
2333	
2334	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2335	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
2336	  localization]
2337	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
2338	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
2339	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
2340	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
2341	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
2342	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
2343	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
2344	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
2345	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
2346	  \[
2347	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
2348	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
2349	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
2350	  \]
2351	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
2352	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
2353	
2354	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
2355	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
2356	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
2357	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
2358	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
2359	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
2360	\end{lemma}
2361	
2362	\begin{proof}
2363	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
2364	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
2365	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
2366	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
2367	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
2368	
2369	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
2370	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
2371	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
2372	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
2373	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
2374	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
2375	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
2376	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
2377	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
2378	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
2379	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
2380	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
2381	\end{proof}
2382	
2383	\begin{lemma}[Sections on a basic open localize the affine ring]
2384	  \label{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2385	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen}
2386	  \mathlibok
2387	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2388	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).}
2389	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open, and
2390	  \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then the restriction map
2391	  \(\Gamma(X, U) \to \Gamma\bigl(X,\, D(f)\bigr)\) on the basic open
2392	  \(D(f) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,f\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(f))\) as the
2393	  localization of \(\Gamma(X, U)\) away from \(f\); that is, it is
2394	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\,f\), so
2395	  \(\Gamma(X, D(f)) = (\Gamma(X, U))_f\) as a \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-algebra.
2396	\end{lemma}
2397	
2398	\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherent sections on a basic open are the localized module]
2399	  \label{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
2400	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen}
2401	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2402	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes" (chapter tag 020J), \S 7
2403	  % "Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines", Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves},
2404	  % item (4) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691-L702).
2405	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2406	  % "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
2407	  %  be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
2408	  %  associated to $M$.
2409	  %  [...]
2410	  %  \item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
2411	  %  as an $R_f$-module."
2412	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves
2413	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
2414	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a \emph{quasi-coherent} sheaf
2415	  of modules on \(X\). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
2416	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
2417	  \begin{enumerate}
2418	    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
2419	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
2420	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
2421	    \item the section-restriction map
2422	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
2423	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
2424	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
2425	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
2426	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).
2427	  \end{enumerate}
2428	\end{lemma}
2429	
2430	\begin{proof}
2431	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2432	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
2433	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
2434	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
2435	
2436	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
2437	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
2438	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
2439	  \(N\) (no finiteness of \(N\) is required for this step). The Spec-local
2440	  computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
2441	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
2442	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
2443	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
2444	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
2445	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
2446	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
2447	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)
2448	  corresponds to the standard open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) -- shows
2449	  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
2450	  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
2451	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
2452	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
2453	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.
2454	\end{proof}

2462	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator, lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le,
2463	    def:schematic_support_immersion}

2465	  % Polynomials" (read from

2467	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2468	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,

2472	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2473	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\). The
2474	  \emph{schematic support} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the closed subscheme of \(X\)
2475	  cut out by its annihilator ideal sheaf (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}):
2476	  \[
2477	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F})
2478	    \;=\; V\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\bigr) \;\hookrightarrow\; X,
2479	  \]
2480	  equipped with its canonical closed immersion into \(X\). This realises
2481	  Nitsure's ``schematic support of \(\mathcal{F}\)'' as a closed subscheme of
2482	  \(X\).

2485	\begin{definition}\leanok
2486	[Schematic-support immersion]
2487	  \label{def:schematic_support_immersion}
2488	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.schematicSupportι}
2489	  \uses{def:schematic_support, def:modules_annihilator}
2490	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\). The
2491	  \emph{schematic-support immersion} is the canonical closed immersion
2492	  \[
2493	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F}) \;\hookrightarrow\; X
2494	  \]
2495	  of the schematic support (\cref{def:schematic_support}) into the ambient
2496	  scheme, namely the subscheme immersion of the annihilator ideal sheaf
2497	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}). It is a
2498	  quasi-compact preimmersion onto the support, and it is the morphism whose
2499	  composite with a base morphism \(f : X \to S\) defines proper support
2500	  (\cref{def:has_proper_support}). Proved directly in Lean.
2501	\end{definition}

2503	\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]
2504	  \label{lem:isProper_mathlib}
2505	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper}
2506	  \mathlibok
2507	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2508	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper}).}
2509	  A morphism \(f : X \to S\) of schemes is \emph{proper} iff it is separated,
2510	  universally closed, and locally of finite type. Properness is stable under base
2511	  change: if \(f : X \to S\) is proper and \(g : T \to S\) is any morphism then
2512	  the base change \(X \times_S T \to T\) is proper. Consequently the base-change
2513	  clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by base-change'')
2514	  applies directly to the proper-support condition below.
2515	\end{lemma}

2517	\begin{definition}
2518	
2519	\leanok
2520	[Proper support over the base]
2521	  \label{def:has_proper_support}
2522	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.HasProperSupport}
2523	  \uses{def:schematic_support, def:schematic_support_immersion, lem:isProper_mathlib}
2524	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors $\Hilb_{X/S}$ and
2525	  % $\Quot_{E/X/S}$" (read from
2526	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L407-L409
2527	  % + L418-L419).
2528	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2529	  % "(i) a coherent sheaf $\F$ on $X_T = X\times_ST$
2530	  %  such that the schematic support of $\F$ is proper over $T$
2531	  %  and $\F$ is flat over $T$, together with
2532	  %  [...]
2533	  %  Then as properness and flatness are preserved
2534	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,"
2535	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2536	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
2537	  \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism. We say \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{has proper support
2538	  over \(S\) (along \(f\))} when the composite morphism
2539	  \[
2540	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F}) \hookrightarrow X \xrightarrow{f} S
2541	  \]
2542	  is proper (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}). Since properness is stable under base
2543	  change (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}), this condition is preserved by pull-back
2544	  along any \(S'\to S\), which is exactly what the well-definedness of the Quot
2545	  functor's pullback action requires.

2552	  \label{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}

2557	  % + L584-L585).

2559	  % "$\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
2560	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
2561	  %  [...]
2562	  %  the set of all equivalence classes $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients

2564	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2565	  Let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
2566	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). We say \(\mathcal{M}\) is \emph{locally free of rank
2567	  \(d\)} when there exists an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) together with

2576	
2577	\begin{definition}\leanok
2578	[Quot functor]
2579	  \label{def:quot_functor}
2580	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor}
2581	  \uses{def:hilbert_polynomial, def:has_proper_support}
2582	
2583	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) and
2584	  % \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\)" (read from
2585	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L394-L433
2586	  % + L481-L498).
2587	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2588	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(X\to S\) be a finite type
2589	  %  scheme over it. Let \(E\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\).
2590	  %  Let \(Sch_S\) denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
2591	  %  over \(S\). For any \(T\to S\) in \(Sch_S\), a \textbf{family of quotients of \(E\)
2592	  %  parametrised by \(T\)} will mean
2593	  %  a pair \((\F,q)\) consisting of
2594	  %
2595	  %  (i) a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on \(X_T = X\times_ST\)
2596	  %  such that the schematic support of \(\F\) is proper over \(T\)
2597	  %  and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\), together with
2598	  %
2599	  %  (ii) a surjective \({\mathcal O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism of sheaves
2600	  %  \(q:E_T  \to \F\) where \(E_T\) is the pull-back of \(E\) under
2601	  %  the projection \(X_T \to X\).
2602	  %
2603	  %  Two such families \((\F,q)\) and \((\F,q)\) parametrised by \(T\) will
2604	  %  be regarded as equivalent if \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\)),
2605	  %  and \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) will denote
2606	  %  an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
2607	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,
2608	  %  the pull-back of \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) under an \(S\)-morphism \(T'\to T\) is
2609	  %  well-defined, which gives
2610	  %  a set-valued contravariant functor
2611	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets\) under which
2612	  %  \(\)T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}\(\)
2613	  %  [...]
2614	  %  This shows that the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\) naturally decomposes
2615	  %  as a co-product
2616	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\(\)
2617	  %  where for any polynomial \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\), the functor
2618	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) associates
2619	  %  to any \(T\) the set of all equivalence classes of
2620	  %  families \(\langle \F,q \rangle\) such that at each \(t\in T\) the
2621	  %  Hilbert polynomial of the restriction \(\F_t\), calculated
2622	  %  using the pull-back of \(L\), is \({\Phi}\)."
2623	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2624	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
2625	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
2626	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
2627	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
2628	  \[
2629	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
2630	  \]
2631	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
2632	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
2633	  \begin{enumerate}
2634	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
2635	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) (\cref{def:has_proper_support}) and
2636	      which is flat over \(T\);
2637	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
2638	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
2639	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
2640	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
2641	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
2642	      \(\Phi\).
2643	  \end{enumerate}
2644	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
2645	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
2646	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
2647	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
2648	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
2649	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
2650	\end{definition}

2652	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
2653	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
2654	
2655	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
2656	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
2657	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
2658	linear algebra), we package the construction here.
2659	
2660	\begin{definition}\leanok
2661	[Grassmannian scheme]
2662	  \label{def:grassmannian_scheme}
2663	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian}
2664	  \uses{def:quot_functor, def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
2665	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises~(2)" and
2666	  % "Construction of Grassmannian" (read from
2667	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L599
2668	  % + L773-L850).
2669	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2670	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), an explicit construction
2671	  %  the Grassmannian scheme
2672	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
2673	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
2674	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
2675	  %  has been given at the end of this section. [...]
2676	  %  Show that \(\grass(r,d)\) together with the quotient
2677	  %  \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) represents the
2678	  %  contravariant functor
2679	  %  \(\)\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}\(\)
2680	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any \(T\) the
2681	  %  set of all equivalence classes
2682	  %  \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients \(q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F\)
2683	  %  where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\).
2684	  %  [...]
2685	  %  Using the above show that, more generally, if \(S\) is a scheme and
2686	  %  \(E\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\),
2687	  %  the functor \(\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}\)
2688	  %  on all \(S\)-schemes which
2689	  %  by definition associates to any \(T\)
2690	  %  the set of all equivalence classes \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients
2691	  %  \(q: E_T \to \F\) where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\),
2692	  %  is representable. The representing scheme
2693	  %  is denoted by \(\grass(E,d)\) and is called the rank \(d\)
2694	  %  relative Grassmannian of \(E\) over \(S\)."
2695	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) and "Construction of
2696	  Grassmannian" (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2697	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(V\) be a locally free
2698	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the
2699	  \emph{Grassmannian of rank-\(d\) quotients of \(V\)} is the functor
2700	  \[
2701	    \mathrm{Grass}(V, d) : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set},
2702	    \qquad
2703	    T \longmapsto \{ \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle :
2704	    q : V_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F},\; \mathcal{F}
2705	    \text{ locally free of rank } d \}
2706	  \]
2707	  with equivalence \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\); the rank-\(d\) local freeness of
2708	  \(\mathcal{F}\) is as in \cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}. Concretely,
2709	  \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) = \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\): the Quot functor
2710	  (\cref{def:quot_functor}) in the special case \(X = S\), \(E = V\), constant
2711	  Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\).
2712	\end{definition}
